proposals.title: Protocol Inversion Reveals Cryptic Sperm Subpopulations: Linking Cryopreservation Sequence to Heterogeneous Biophysical Resilience in Boar Spermatozoa
proposals.motivation: Cryopreservation induces heterogeneous damage in boar sperm, a major bottleneck for artificial insemination and genetic management. Pre-2026, standard protocols apply synthetic liquid crystal (SLC) media after cooling, potentially masking pre-existing subpopulation vulnerabilities. Understanding how procedural sequence acts as a selective filter is crucial for moving beyond population-averaged metrics to subpopulation-specific preservation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Inverting the standard cryopreservation protocol—applying SLC before cooling versus after—will selectively expose distinct, pre-existing subpopulations of boar spermatozoa based on differential resilience to cold shock versus SLC-induced osmotic stress, as revealed by single-cell analysis.
proposals.method: We will collect and process boar semen using standard NCSU-23 medium and centrifugation protocols. Two cryopreservation arms will be compared: 1) Standard: cooling to 5°C followed by SLC addition and freezing; 2) Inverted: SLC addition at 37°C followed by cooling and freezing. Post-thaw, high-throughput single-cell analyses will be employed: computer-assisted sperm analysis (CASA) for kinematics, flow cytometry with Yo-Pro-1/PI for membrane integrity, and chromatin dispersion (SCD) assays. Data will be analyzed using multivariate clustering (e.g., k-means, principal component analysis) to identify damage phenotype cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, mechanistic link between procedural sequence and the unmasking of cryptic sperm heterogeneity. It will provide a functional classification of sperm based on survival trajectory, moving beyond mean survival rates. The findings will guide the rational design of tailored preservation protocols targeting specific resilient subpopulations to improve fertility outcomes.
proposals.novelty: The study innovates by using protocol inversion not merely to optimize survival, but as a diagnostic tool to probe inherent biophysical heterogeneity. This approach is distinct from standard comparative cryobiology, as it treats the protocol sequence itself as an experimental variable to dissect subpopulation responses.

proposals.title: Reversing the Cooling-Capacitation Sequence: Evaluating the Impact of Single-Layer Centrifugation Prior to Chilled Storage on Boar Sperm Quality and Chromatin Integrity
proposals.motivation: Standard protocols for boar semen preservation involve cooling ejaculates to 17°C before applying sperm selection techniques like Single-Layer Centrifugation (SLC). This cooling step may induce cold shock, destabilizing sperm membranes and chromatin, thereby reducing the efficacy of subsequent SLC and the longevity of stored sperm. Optimizing the order of processing steps is critical for improving the fertility outcomes of artificial insemination doses in swine production.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying SLC to neat semen at ambient temperature prior to the cooling and storage phase will select a more robust sperm population, leading to better preservation of sperm motility, membrane integrity, and chromatin structure during extended liquid storage compared to the conventional protocol of cooling before SLC.
proposals.method: Fresh boar ejaculates will be split. The control aliquot will be cooled to 17°C, held for 3 hours, then processed by SLC using a species-specific medium (e.g., Androcoll-P). The experimental aliquot will undergo SLC immediately post-collection at room temperature (22°C). Both resulting sperm pellets will be resuspended in a commercial long-term extender (e.g., Beltsville Thawing Solution), cooled to 17°C, and stored for 0, 24, 48, and 72 hours. Assessments will include computer-assisted sperm analysis (CASA) for motility, flow cytometry with SYBR-14/PI for viability, and chromatin structure assay (SCSA) using acridine orange to measure DNA fragmentation index (%DFI).
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a fundamental procedural assumption in boar semen processing, providing evidence-based guidance for protocol optimization. It will contribute to understanding how the timing of sperm selection interacts with temperature stress to affect sperm resilience, potentially leading to improved storage protocols that better maintain sperm fertilizing potential.
proposals.novelty: While SLC is established for selecting high-quality sperm, its application *before* the cooling phase of preservation is a deliberate inversion of the standard sequence. This approach is non-obvious as it challenges the routine practice of immediate semen cooling for metabolic inhibition, proposing that early selection of a robust cohort may offer greater protection against storage-associated damage.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Protective Effect of a Short-Term Liquid Storage Medium on Boar Spermatozoa: A Translational Study Using Human Oxidative Stress Biomarkers
proposals.motivation: Oxidative stress is a major cause of sperm dysfunction in both human and porcine fertility. While assays for reactive oxygen species (ROS) and total antioxidant capacity (TAC) are standard in human andrology, their systematic application to evaluate commercial boar semen extenders is limited. Validating these human clinical biomarkers in the boar model can provide a direct, quantitative measure of extender efficacy, crucial for improving semen storage protocols and reproductive outcomes in swine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific short-term liquid storage (SLC) medium will significantly reduce oxidative damage and improve redox balance in boar spermatozoa during in vitro storage, as measured by established human oxidative stress biomarkers, and that these improvements will correlate with maintained sperm chromatin integrity and in vitro capacitation potential.
proposals.method: Fresh boar ejaculates will be processed using standard NCSU-based medium and centrifugation protocols, then diluted in the test SLC medium and a control extender. At 0, 24, and 72 hours of storage at 17°C, samples will be analyzed. Oxidative stress will be quantified using the human-validated chemiluminescence assay for ROS (e.g., luminol-peroxidase) and a colorimetric kit for TAC. Parallel assessments will include flow cytometric evaluation of sperm viability (SYBR-14/PI), chromatin status (acridine orange test), and capacitation-like changes (chlortetracycline staining and protein tyrosine phosphorylation via immunoblotting). Fertility correlations will be assessed using an in vitro sperm-oocyte binding assay.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for assessing boar semen extenders using clinically relevant oxidative stress metrics. It will directly link the antioxidant properties of an SLC medium to key sperm quality parameters, including chromatin stability and capacitation ability. The findings will offer a translatable model for refining storage solutions to enhance sperm longevity and fertility potential in artificial insemination programs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of standardized human infertility biomarkers (ROS/TAC assays) to the boar model for the specific evaluation of a commercial semen extender, creating a direct bridge between clinical andrology and animal reproductive technology that has not been fully explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A dual-selection microfluidic platform integrating hyaluronic acid binding and colloid centrifugation for the enrichment of high-integrity sperm from endangered donkey semen
proposals.motivation: The conservation of endangered donkey breeds is hampered by limited access to high-quality semen for assisted reproduction and biobanking. Conventional sperm processing methods (e.g., density gradient centrifugation) are inefficient for small, precious ejaculates and do not directly select for functional maturity and DNA integrity. There is a critical need for a micro-volume, integrated selection technology that maximizes the yield of viable, competent spermatozoa from rare samples.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sequential selection of endangered donkey sperm via microfluidic HA-binding, followed by a miniaturized Single-Layer Colloid (SLC) gradient, will isolate a sperm subpopulation with superior chromatin integrity, viability, and in vitro functional competence compared to samples processed by standard methods.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a PDMS-glass microfluidic device featuring an upstream channel with a covalently bound HA surface for selective binding of mature sperm. Unbound cells will be washed away, and bound sperm will be enzymatically released. This eluate will be introduced into a downstream chamber containing a stabilized, miniaturized SLC gradient (e.g., 65% SilSelect) for density-based selection. Sperm parameters (motility, viability, acrosome status via FITC-PNA, chromatin integrity via SCSA or Toluidine Blue) will be compared between raw semen, HA-selected, and dual-selected (HA+SLC) populations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, integrated microfluidic protocol specifically tailored for the biobanking of endangered equids. It will provide the first empirical data on the applicability and synergistic benefits of combining HA-binding with colloid centrifugation in a micro-volume format, directly contributing to the toolkit for wildlife conservation andrology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of two proven sperm selection principles (HA-binding and SLC) into a single, continuous microfluidic workflow designed for minimal sample volumes, which has not been reported for any species, and its first application to the pressing challenge of endangered donkey semen cryopreservation.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Micro-Single Layer Centrifugation (μSLC) Protocol Using Small-Volume Silane-Coated Silica Colloids for Processing Low-Sperm-Count Ejaculates from Endangered Donkey Breeds
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the Single Layer Centrifugation (SLC) technique with silane-coated silica colloids (e.g., Androcoll-P) is established for selecting high-quality, functional boar spermatozoa, improving chromatin integrity and fertility. However, its standard volume requirements (e.g., 15-20 mL colloid) are prohibitive for processing the small, precious ejaculates from endangered donkey breeds, where maximizing the yield of viable sperm from low-count samples is critical for conservation efforts via assisted reproduction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that miniaturizing the SLC protocol using significantly reduced volumes of a species-optimized silane-coated silica colloid will effectively select a high-proportion of motile, morphologically normal, and chromatin-intact donkey spermatozoa from low-concentration ejaculates, without compromising sperm functionality.
proposals.method: We will adapt the established boar SLC protocol for donkey sperm. A species-specific colloid will be formulated based on pre-2026 knowledge of donkey sperm density. The protocol will be miniaturized by scaling down colloid volume to 1-2 mL in narrow 5 mL tubes. Sperm parameters (motility, viability, morphology, and chromatin integrity via Sperm Chromatin Structure Assay - SCSA) will be compared between μSLC-processed samples and controls (centrifugation in a standard medium) using flow cytometry and CASA. The optimal colloid volume, centrifugation force (xg), and time will be determined using a response surface methodology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, low-volume sperm selection protocol tailored for endangered equids, directly enabling more efficient use of scarce genetic material in conservation breeding programs. It will extend the application of colloidal centrifugation principles from livestock production to wildlife conservation, generating crucial pre-freeze and post-thaw quality data for donkey sperm biobanking.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the radical miniaturization of the SLC technique for ultra-low-volume, high-value samples and its specific application to an unstudied, conservation-critical species (donkey), bridging a gap between advanced livestock spermatology and endangered species management.

proposals.title: High-Content Phenotypic and Genomic Profiling of the Single Layer Centrifugation Waste Fraction to Identify Non-Standard Boar Sperm Subpopulations
proposals.motivation: Single Layer Centrifugation (SLC) is a standard method for selecting high-quality boar sperm for artificial insemination, but its waste fraction is typically discarded. This fraction may contain sperm with unconventional but potentially valuable phenotypes, such as unique motility patterns or resilience to specific stressors, which are not prioritized by standard SLC criteria. In cases of recurrent fertilization failure with SLC-selected sperm, this waste fraction represents an untapped reservoir for a second-line, data-driven selection strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: The SLC waste fraction contains subpopulations of sperm with distinct, beneficial phenotypic traits and intact genomic integrity, which can be systematically identified and validated for use as an alternative sperm source.
proposals.method: We will develop a high-content analysis platform using computer-assisted sperm analysis (CASA) and flow cytometry to continuously profile discarded boar sperm for non-standard traits (e.g., hyperactivated motility patterns, membrane fluidity, or oxidative stress resilience). Identified candidate sperm will be individually isolated via fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS). A rapid, targeted genomic screen using quantitative PCR for apoptosis-related genes and the sperm chromatin structure assay (SCSA) will confirm baseline genetic integrity and disqualify sperm with excessive DNA fragmentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, systematic framework for re-evaluating discarded sperm populations, moving beyond rescue towards discovery of alternative functional phenotypes. It will establish a validated protocol for creating a second-line sperm pool, potentially improving fertility outcomes in challenging cases. The data will contribute to a more nuanced understanding of sperm phenotypic diversity and its relationship to SLC selection criteria.
proposals.novelty: This approach fundamentally reframes the SLC waste fraction as a discovery reservoir rather than a simple discard, applying a combined high-content phenotypic and rapid genomic screening pipeline specifically for non-standard trait identification, which is not a focus of current andrology workflows.

proposals.title: Inverse Selection: AI-Driven Morphological Phenotyping to Recover Viable Sperm Subpopulations from the Single-Layer Centrifugation Waste Fraction in Donkeys
proposals.motivation: Single-layer centrifugation (SLC) is a standard technique for selecting high-quality sperm, but it discards a significant fraction of cells, potentially including viable sperm with non-standard morphologies. In species like donkeys, where semen resources can be limited, maximizing the yield from each ejaculate is crucial for genetic management and assisted reproduction. Current post-SLC analysis lacks the granularity to characterize the morphological diversity within the waste fraction, representing a loss of potentially valuable genetic material.
proposals.hypothesis: The SLC waste fraction contains sub-populations of viable spermatozoa with distinct, quantifiable morphological phenotypes that can be identified and recovered using deep learning-based computer vision, thereby expanding the usable sperm pool from a single ejaculate.
proposals.method: Waste fractions from donkey ejaculates processed via SLC will be imaged using Computer-Assisted Sperm Analysis (CASA) and high-throughput microscopy. A convolutional neural network (CNN), such as a ResNet-50 architecture, will be trained on expert-annotated images to perform multi-label classification of sperm head, midpiece, and tail morphology beyond standard WHO criteria. The AI model will segment and phenotype individual cells in the waste, clustering them into subpopulations. Sperm from identified 'recoverable' clusters will be isolated via micromanipulation for functional assays (e.g., membrane integrity, chromatin stability, and in vitro fertilization potential).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel 'inverse selection' pipeline that repurposes discarded sperm based on AI-defined phenotypes, directly increasing usable sperm yield for donkey breeding. It will provide the first detailed morphological map of the SLC waste fraction, revealing phenotype-function relationships for non-standard sperm. The methodology will offer a translatable framework for sperm rescue in other livestock and endangered species.
proposals.novelty: This approach inverts the standard paradigm by applying advanced AI phenotyping not to the selected sperm, but to the discarded fraction to actively recover viable cells, a concept not explored in pre-2026 equid reproduction research. It moves beyond using AI for quality scoring to using it for discovery and rescue of phenotypically distinct subpopulations.

proposals.title: Characterization and Functional Rescue of Viable Spermatozoa from the Discarded Fraction of Single-Layer Centrifugation in Boar Semen
proposals.motivation: Single-layer centrifugation (SLC) with products like Androcoll-P is a standard pre-freezing technique to select high-quality, morphologically normal boar sperm. However, the discarded waste fraction, containing up to 70-80% of the initial sample, is presumed non-viable and is routinely disposed of. This represents a significant potential loss of genetic material and functional spermatozoa that may possess different, yet valuable, physiological characteristics relevant for fertility or assisted reproduction.
proposals.hypothesis: The SLC waste fraction contains a subpopulation of viable spermatozoa with distinct chromatin integrity, capacitation status, and kinematic profiles compared to the SLC-selected population, and these cells can be rescued and functionally validated for use in assisted reproductive technologies.
proposals.method: We will use fresh boar ejaculates and process them via SLC with a colloidal silane-coated silica gradient (Androcoll-P). The waste fraction will be recovered and analyzed using a multi-parametric flow cytometry panel (SYBR-14/PI for viability, Yo-Pro-1 for early apoptosis, M540/CTC for capacitation status, and chromomycin A3 for chromatin compaction). Computer-assisted sperm analysis (CASA) will assess motility kinematics. A subset of waste-fraction sperm, identified as viable by flow sorting, will be used for in vitro fertilization (IVF) of in vitro-matured porcine oocytes to assess blastocyst formation rates, comparing outcomes to standard SLC-selected sperm.
proposals.contribution: This work will systematically map the phenotypic diversity within the SLC waste fraction, challenging the binary view of 'selected' vs. 'waste' sperm. It will provide a methodology for potentially increasing the yield of usable sperm from valuable ejaculates, which is critical for genomics and endangered breed conservation. The findings could lead to optimized SLC protocols or secondary processing methods to salvage functional sperm.
proposals.novelty: While SLC is well-studied for its selected population, a comprehensive, multi-parametric analysis of the discarded fraction to identify and functionally rescue viable spermatozoa with alternative physiological states is novel. This 'inverse problem' approach re-frames the waste fraction as a source of biological diversity rather than mere debris.

proposals.title: Development of an Integrated Microfluidic Lab-on-a-Chip for Automated Boar Sperm Processing and Real-Time Viability Assessment
proposals.motivation: Current boar sperm processing for artificial insemination involves sequential, manual steps of centrifugation, swim-up, and separate viability assays, which are time-consuming, induce oxidative stress, and provide delayed results. This fragmentation hinders the rapid, objective selection of high-fertility spermatozoa, a critical need in both commercial pig production and assisted reproductive technologies. An integrated system that combines gentle, efficient sperm selection with immediate functional analysis would minimize handling artifacts and improve decision-making for sperm dose preparation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single microfluidic platform can effectively isolate motile, morphologically normal boar spermatozoa from raw semen and subsequently provide real-time, multi-parameter functional assessment of their capacitation status and membrane integrity, correlating strongly with traditional fertility markers.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a PDMS-glass microfluidic chip with two integrated modules. The first module will utilize a passive, boundary-driven separation principle (e.g., a labyrinthine channel or deterministic lateral displacement array) to isolate progressively motile sperm from seminal plasma, debris, and immotile cells based on their size and motility. The second module will consist of functional assessment zones with embedded biosensors: fluorescence-based detection using a calcium-sensitive dye (Fluo-4 AM) to monitor capacitation-associated calcium flux, and an electrochemical sensor to assess metabolic activity via redox cycling. Sperm trajectory and velocity will be tracked in real-time using an on-chip high-speed imaging system.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a proof-of-concept, automated platform that significantly reduces processing time and manual intervention for boar sperm analysis. It will provide a novel, correlative dataset linking real-time, on-chip functional parameters (motility, calcium flux, metabolism) with standard laboratory measures of sperm chromatin integrity and in vitro fertilizing capacity. The proposed chip could serve as a foundational technology for next-generation, objective semen analysis in livestock and human andrology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of a gentle, motility-based selection module with immediate, multi-parametric functional biosensing in a continuous microfluidic workflow, adapted from environmental monitoring concepts. This moves beyond existing microfluidic sperm sorters or standalone sensors by creating a unified 'sample-to-answer' system for comprehensive sperm health profiling.

proposals.title: Label-free metabolic profiling of boar spermatozoa during in vitro capacitation using confocal Raman spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: Current assessments of sperm metabolism and viability for artificial insemination rely on fluorophore-based assays (e.g., flow cytometry), which are invasive, can alter cell function, and require complex sample preparation. A non-invasive, label-free method is needed to provide real-time, biochemical insights into sperm physiology, particularly during the critical process of capacitation, which directly impacts fertility outcomes in swine production.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Raman spectroscopy can detect specific, quantifiable biochemical signatures (e.g., lipid remodeling, protein secondary structure changes, redox state) in individual boar spermatozoa that correlate with metabolic status and successful in vitro capacitation, providing a superior predictive marker for fertility compared to traditional motility and membrane integrity assays.
proposals.method: We will use a confocal Raman microspectroscopy system (e.g., 785 nm laser) to acquire spectra from individual sperm cells from fresh boar ejaculates processed via standard NCSU-23 medium and centrifugation protocols. Sperm will be analyzed pre- and post-in vitro capacitation. Spectral data will be processed using baseline correction, vector normalization, and principal component analysis (PCA) to identify key spectral bands. Partial Least Squares-Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA) will be used to build a classification model correlating Raman profiles with functional outcomes (motility, viability via flow cytometry control, and chromatin status).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish Raman spectroscopy as a novel, non-destructive analytical tool for andrology, providing a direct biochemical fingerprint of sperm metabolic fitness. It will contribute a new set of spectral biomarkers for boar sperm capacitation and viability, potentially leading to more objective and rapid semen quality assessment protocols for the swine industry.
proposals.novelty: While Raman spectroscopy has been explored for human sperm, its application to boar sperm capacitation is novel, and the proposed integration of spectral data with advanced chemometrics (PLS-DA) to predict functional states moves beyond simple spectral observation to a predictive, diagnostic framework.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of an Automated, High-Throughput System for Boar Sperm Clean-Up and Capacitation Assessment in Commercial AI Centers
proposals.motivation: Commercial pig AI centers process large volumes of semen daily, but current sperm clean-up protocols (e.g., density gradient centrifugation) are labor-intensive, time-consuming, and introduce operator-dependent variability. This bottleneck limits throughput and can compromise sperm quality, directly impacting fertility outcomes. Automating this process is critical for scaling up operations while maintaining consistent, high-quality sperm doses for artificial insemination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an automated, closed-system platform for boar sperm processing will yield sperm populations with superior and more consistent post-thaw chromatin integrity and capacitation status compared to standard manual protocols, thereby improving predicted fertility.
proposals.method: We will adapt a liquid handling robotic platform (e.g., Hamilton STAR) to automate the Percoll or Accudenz density gradient centrifugation protocol. The system will integrate a flow cytometer for inline, real-time quality assessment, measuring sperm viability (SYBR-14/PI), acrosome status (PNA-FITC), and chromatin integrity (SCSA via acridine orange). Machine learning algorithms (e.g., a Random Forest classifier) will be trained on pre-2026 datasets to correlate these automated readouts with traditional manual assessments and in vitro fertilization rates, enabling real-time process optimization and quality control.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, scalable prototype for automated boar sperm processing, directly addressing the throughput needs of the swine industry. It will provide a novel dataset linking automated high-throughput sperm quality parameters (especially chromatin stability and capacitation) to fertility potential, establishing new standards for AI center efficiency and semen dose consistency.
proposals.novelty: While robotics exist in labs, their application for high-volume, farm-level boar semen processing is unexplored. The novelty lies in the closed-loop integration of automated physical processing with real-time, multi-parameter flow cytometric feedback and machine learning-based quality prediction, tailored specifically for the boar ejaculate's unique physiological characteristics.

proposals.title: Inverse Selection for Cryo-Resilience: Identifying and Characterizing a Boar Sperm Subpopulation with Superior Post-Thaw Chromatin Integrity and Fertilizing Potential
proposals.motivation: Cryopreservation severely damages boar sperm, reducing fertility and limiting the use of artificial insemination with frozen doses. Current selection methods (e.g., post-thaw motility, morphology) identify sperm that appear normal but may still harbor latent, cryo-induced damage like chromatin destabilization, which is linked to poor embryo development. A paradigm shift from selecting 'normal' sperm to proactively isolating those inherently resilient to cryo-stress is needed to improve outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: A subpopulation of boar spermatozoa exists that is intrinsically resilient to cryopreservation-induced stress, and this resilience is characterized by superior maintenance of chromatin structure and capacitation ability post-thaw compared to sperm selected by conventional parameters.
proposals.method: We will employ an inverse selection strategy using pre-2026 flow cytometric methods. Fresh boar ejaculates will be split: one aliquot cryopreserved using standard NCSU-based protocols, another serves as a fresh control. Post-thaw, sperm will be simultaneously labeled for viability (SYBR-14/PI), chromatin integrity (SCSA: acridine orange for DNA fragmentation index, DFI), and capacitation status (chlortetracycline fluorescence, CTC). Using high-speed cell sorting, we will isolate two populations from the same thawed sample: 1) Conventionally Selected: viable, motile sperm. 2) Resilience-Selected: viable sperm with the lowest DFI (most chromatin-stable). Sorted subpopulations will be assessed for in vitro fertilizing ability and subsequent embryo development.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, function-based selection framework for boar sperm cryopreservation, moving beyond descriptive post-thaw metrics. It will directly link pre-freeze resilience markers (like inherent chromatin stability) to post-thaw function, providing a targeted phenotype for genetic or bioprocessing optimization to enhance frozen semen fertility.
proposals.novelty: Instead of assessing and discarding damaged sperm post-thaw, we proactively use the cryopreservation process as a stress test to identify and isolate the most resilient subpopulation based on chromatin integrity, a functional trait not captured by standard motility or morphology checks.

proposals.title: Comprehensive Functional Profiling of Post-Thaw Boar Sperm: Integrating Motility Kinematics, Capacitation Status, and Chromatin Integrity to Predict Fertility
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the primary endpoint for evaluating cryopreserved boar sperm is membrane integrity (e.g., SYBR-14/PI staining), which is a poor predictor of field fertility. This discrepancy creates significant economic inefficiency in swine AI. A more complete, functional assessment is needed to understand the true biological cost of cryopreservation on sperm capacitation ability and chromatin stability, which are critical for successful fertilization and embryo development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-parametric functional assessment, combining kinematic analysis, capacitation-dependent tyrosine phosphorylation patterns, and sperm chromatin structure assay (SCSA), will reveal sub-lethal cryodamage not detected by membrane integrity tests and provide a superior predictor of in vitro fertilization (IVF) outcomes and farrowing rates.
proposals.method: Semen from high-fertility boars will be cryopreserved using a standard NCSU-based medium and protocol. Post-thaw, samples will be assessed for: 1) membrane integrity (flow cytometry with SYBR-14/PI), 2) total and progressive motility with kinematic parameters (CASA system), 3) acrosome integrity (FITC-PNA/PI flow cytometry), 4) capacitation status via immunodetection of tyrosine phosphorylation patterns, and 5) chromatin damage (SCSA using acridine orange and flow cytometry). Functional fertility will be tested using a standardized porcine IVF system, correlating laboratory parameters with cleavage and blastocyst rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a standardized, multi-faceted protocol for the functional evaluation of cryopreserved boar sperm, moving the field beyond simple viability. It will directly link specific cryodamage signatures (e.g., altered phosphorylation, high DNA fragmentation index) to failed fertilization or early embryonic development, providing AI centers with actionable criteria for batch selection.
proposals.novelty: While individual assays exist, their integrated application to define a 'functional viability score' for post-thaw boar sperm is novel. This approach specifically connects the pre-2026 themes of capacitation and chromatin integrity with practical fertility outcomes, creating a holistic damage profile beyond the standard clinical focus on membrane integrity.

proposals.title: Identification of Oviductal Fluid Proteomic and Ionic Cues Triggering Boar Sperm Release from the Isthmic Oviductal Reservoir
proposals.motivation: In pigs, sperm are held in a functional reservoir within the isthmic oviductal fluid (IOF) mucus prior to ovulation, ensuring timely fertilization. The precise molecular signals from the oviduct that trigger sperm release post-ovulation remain poorly characterized, representing a critical gap in understanding the regulation of in vivo fertility. Elucidating these triggers is essential for improving artificial insemination protocols and diagnosing unexplained subfertility linked to failed sperm release.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that post-ovulatory changes in the oviductal fluid proteome and ionic composition (specifically calcium and bicarbonate fluxes) disrupt sperm-oviduct binding and induce hyperactivation, serving as the primary physiological trigger for sperm release from the isthmic reservoir.
proposals.method: We will collect IOF from pre- and post-ovulatory porcine oviducts ex vivo. Using quantitative LC-MS/MS proteomics, we will identify differentially abundant proteins. Concurrently, ion-selective electrode analysis will quantify changes in Ca2+ and HCO3- concentrations. The functional role of candidate triggers will be tested using a validated in vitro sperm-oviduct epithelial cell co-culture binding assay, where pre-bound capacitated boar sperm are exposed to defined post-ovulatory IOF or synthetic medium mimicking the identified changes, with sperm release and kinematic parameters (via CASA) measured.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive profile of post-ovulatory changes in the porcine oviductal fluid that are functionally linked to sperm release. It will identify specific protein and ionic candidates as key regulators of sperm reservoir function, offering new biomarkers for sperm selection and fertility diagnostics. The findings will directly inform the refinement of in vitro fertilization and semen storage media to better mimic these physiological events.
proposals.novelty: While prior work established the reservoir's existence and general capacitation requirements, this study directly links specific, quantified oviductal fluid compositional shifts to the physical release of sperm, moving beyond correlation to functional testing of defined molecular triggers in a porcine model.

proposals.title: Disconnect between in vitro sperm quality markers and in vivo fertility: A boar sperm capacitation study challenging the predictive value of cervical AI outcomes
proposals.motivation: In swine production, cervical artificial insemination (AI) is predominant, and semen extenders are optimized to maintain in vitro sperm motility and membrane integrity. However, the assumption that these improved in vitro parameters directly translate to higher field fertility is often untested. This gap leads to potential misallocation of resources in developing semen technologies that may not address the key in vivo bottlenecks to fertilization success.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific in vitro markers of boar sperm capacitation status (e.g., protein tyrosine phosphorylation patterns, cholesterol efflux) will show a stronger correlation with cervical AI fertility outcomes than conventional quality parameters (motility, membrane integrity) alone, and that improvements in the latter do not guarantee enhanced pregnancy rates.
proposals.method: Semen from 30 boars will be processed using standard NCSU-23 extender and subjected to controlled in vitro capacitation. Samples will be assessed pre- and post-capacitation for: 1) computer-assisted sperm analysis (CASA) for motility, 2) flow cytometry for membrane status (SYBR-14/PI, Merocyanine 540) and capacitation-associated changes (anti-phosphotyrosine immunofluorescence, chlortetracycline staining), and 3) chromatin integrity (sperm chromatin structure assay, SCSA). Each processed dose will be used for cervical AI in a standardized, high-parity sow herd (n=15 inseminations per boar). Fertility outcomes (farrowing rate, litter size) will be correlated with laboratory parameters using multivariate regression and principal component analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct, quantitative assessment of which laboratory assays are most predictive of field fertility for cervical AI in swine. It will challenge the field to refine semen quality assessment beyond motility, focusing on functional capacitation readiness. The results will guide the development of more relevant in vitro tests and extender formulations aimed at true fertility improvement.
proposals.novelty: While many studies correlate single in vitro parameters with fertility, this proposal systematically tests the core assumption that improving standard motility/membrane metrics necessarily improves pregnancy rates, using a multi-parameter, capacitation-focused approach within a rigorous, controlled field trial design specific to cervical AI.

proposals.title: Fertility Outcomes of Laparoscopic Intrauterine Insemination with Boar Spermatozoa Supplemented with Defined Seminal Plasma Fractions: A Translational Study
proposals.motivation: In swine AI, the shift to low-dose intrauterine insemination (IUI) bypasses the cervix, omitting the natural exposure of sperm to seminal plasma (SP) which is known to modulate sperm capacitation and immune responses in the female tract. Pre-2026 in vitro studies, including those from our group, show specific SP fractions can enhance sperm chromatin stability and capacitation status, but their efficacy in vivo remains untested. Translating these in vitro benefits to practical fertility outcomes is critical for optimizing porcine reproductive technologies and improving the efficiency of genetic dissemination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that supplementing extended boar spermatozoa with specific, functionally defined seminal plasma fractions (e.g., those rich in spermadhesins or antioxidants) prior to laparoscopic IUI will improve farrowing rates and litter sizes compared to standard extended semen, by enhancing sperm survival, capacitation synchrony, and early embryo signaling.
proposals.method: We will fractionate pooled boar seminal plasma using fast protein liquid chromatography (FPLC) based on established pre-2026 protocols to isolate fractions previously associated with in vitro benefits (e.g., heparin-binding proteins for capacitation modulation, antioxidant-rich fractions). Extended sperm doses for laparoscopic IUI will be prepared using the NCSU-23 medium, with centrifugation and resuspension in extender containing the target fraction or a control. Multiparous sows will be assigned to treatment groups: 1) Standard extended semen (control), 2) Semen + Fraction A (capacitation-modulating), 3) Semen + Fraction B (chromatin-protective). Fertility outcomes (farrowing rate, total born) will be recorded and analyzed using chi-square and ANOVA.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence linking defined seminal plasma fractions to measurable fertility gains in a practical porcine IUI system. It will move the field beyond correlative in vitro assays to establish causal, fraction-specific roles in reproductive success. The results could directly inform the formulation of next-generation semen extenders tailored for low-dose, deep-uterine insemination protocols.
proposals.novelty: While SP effects are well-studied in vitro and with conventional AI, testing purified fractions in the context of laparoscopic IUI is novel, as it isolates the biochemical contribution of SP from its physical role in cervical transit. This approach directly tests the hypothesis that specific SP components are necessary for fertility even when sperm are deposited directly at the utero-tubal junction.

proposals.title: Development and Standardization of a Scalable Process for the Production of Boar Seminal Plasma Fractions to Enhance Sperm Cryopreservation and In Vitro Capacitation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including the investigator's own work, has established that seminal plasma contains bioactive factors crucial for sperm function, chromatin stability, and capacitation in boars. However, the application of these factors in commercial semen processing is limited by the lack of standardized, large-scale production methods for active seminal plasma fractions. This bottleneck hinders the development of next-generation extenders that could improve fertility outcomes in artificial insemination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a scalable fractionation protocol, based on tangential flow filtration and chromatographic techniques, can be developed to consistently produce seminal plasma sub-fractions that retain bioactivity, thereby improving post-thaw sperm quality and supporting in vitro capacitation.
proposals.method: The method will involve collecting boar seminal plasma via established centrifugation protocols. We will develop a scalable purification pipeline using tangential flow filtration (TFF) systems for initial volume reduction and diafiltration, followed by sequential chromatographic steps (e.g., ion-exchange and size-exclusion) to isolate specific protein-rich fractions. Bioactivity will be assessed using pre-2026 standard assays: flow cytometric evaluation of sperm viability, chromatin integrity (SCSA), and capacitation status (chlortetracycline assay) after adding fractions to NCSU-based storage and capacitation media.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, industrial-scale method for producing standardized seminal plasma fractions, directly addressing a key translational gap in animal reproduction. It will contribute a new, commercially viable bioprocessing protocol for the semen industry and generate fundamental knowledge on the stability and function of seminal plasma components during scale-up.
proposals.novelty: While seminal plasma components are well-studied, the systematic engineering of a scalable manufacturing process for these labile biologicals, with a focus on industrial standardization for boar semen, represents a novel and non-obvious application of bioprocessing techniques to animal reproductive biotechnology.

proposals.title: Economic and Fertility Impact of Utilizing the Rest Fraction of Boar Ejaculate for Liquid Artificial Insemination: A Comparative Analysis of Sperm Quality and On-Farm Efficiency
proposals.motivation: In swine AI, the sperm-rich fraction is typically used for liquid semen doses, while the rest fraction is often discarded or processed for frozen semen, representing a potential economic loss. Given the high volume of the rest fraction and its known biochemical differences, a systematic evaluation of its direct use in liquid AI is needed. Determining if this practice is economically optimal requires a direct comparison of fertility outcomes and processing costs against the standard use of the sperm-rich fraction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using the rest fraction of the boar ejaculate for liquid AI doses will be the most economically efficient strategy, as the reduction in fertility per dose will be offset by the significant increase in the total number of viable doses produced per ejaculate, without requiring additional processing for cryopreservation.
proposals.method: Ejaculates from mature boars (n=30) will be manually fractionated into sperm-rich and rest fractions. Each fraction will be processed for liquid storage using a standard NCSU-23 extender protocol, including centrifugation and resuspension. Sperm quality (motility, viability, acrosome integrity, chromatin stability via flow cytometry) and in vitro capacitation status will be assessed at 0, 24, 48, and 72h of storage at 17°C. A large-scale field trial will compare fertility (farrowing rate, litter size) using doses from each fraction. A detailed economic model will integrate sperm dose yield, processing time, storage longevity, and fertility results.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a definitive, data-driven answer on the economic efficiency of rest-fraction utilization, directly informing commercial swine breeding practices. It will contribute novel data on the functional longevity and capacitation dynamics of rest-fraction spermatozoa during liquid storage, linking seminal plasma composition to practical outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While the rest fraction's characteristics are known, its direct, parallel use in liquid AI as a primary economic strategy has not been rigorously tested against the sperm-rich fraction in a combined biological and economic framework. The novelty lies in the integrated assessment of sperm chromatin stability during liquid storage of the rest fraction as a predictor of its economic viability.

proposals.title: Optimization of a Cryopreservation Protocol for Boar Spermatozoa Specifically for Long-Term Genetic Banking: Effects on Chromatin Integrity and Post-Thaw Capacitation Potential
proposals.motivation: Genetic diversity in swine populations is threatened by the consolidation of global breeding lines. While boar semen cryopreservation is established, current protocols are optimized for routine artificial insemination (AI), prioritizing immediate post-thaw motility and membrane integrity for fertility. For long-term gene banking, the primary objective shifts to preserving paternal genomic information with maximal chromatin stability over decades, a parameter not prioritized in AI-focused protocols. A dedicated protocol for genetic preservation is therefore critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a cryopreservation protocol specifically designed for genetic banking, which minimizes oxidative and mechanical stress during processing, will better preserve sperm chromatin structure and the latent potential for capacitation post-long-term storage compared to standard AI-optimized freezing methods.
proposals.method: We will use ejaculates from boars (n=12) and process the sperm-rich P1 fraction. A standard AI freezing protocol (control) will be compared against a 'Gene Banking' protocol featuring gentler centrifugation forces, an antioxidant-supplemented freezing medium (e.g., based on NCSU-23 with added catalase and superoxide dismutase), and a slower cooling curve. Post-thaw, we will assess standard semen quality parameters (motility, viability) and, crucially, chromatin integrity via flow cytometric Sperm Chromatin Structure Assay (SCSA) and Toluidine Blue staining. Capacitation potential will be evaluated via chlortetracycline (CTC) assay after incubation under capacitating conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, purpose-built cryopreservation protocol for swine genetic repositories, directly linking processing methods to key biomarkers for long-term genomic storage (chromatin integrity). It will establish a clear distinction between 'fertility-ready' and 'archive-ready' frozen semen, offering a practical framework for gene banks to maximize the value of stored genetics.
proposals.novelty: While boar semen cryopreservation is well-studied for AI, this proposal explicitly decouples the goal of immediate fertility from long-term genetic preservation, designing and testing a protocol specifically for the latter based on pre-2026 understanding of sperm chromatin vulnerability and capacitation biochemistry.

proposals.title: Fertility Outcomes Following Artificial Insemination with Non-P1 Boar Sperm Fractions: Challenging the Paradigm of Ejaculate Fraction Superiority
proposals.motivation: In boar semen processing, the first, sperm-rich ejaculate fraction (P1) is considered essential for high fertility due to its superior sperm concentration and quality. This paradigm dictates commercial practice, but it limits the efficient use of the entire ejaculate. Challenging this assumption could optimize semen dose production and improve the utilization of genetically valuable sires.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that later, non-P1 ejaculate fractions (P2 and P3), when properly processed and stored, can achieve fertility rates comparable to those of the traditional P1 fraction in commercial artificial insemination (AI) programs.
proposals.method: Ejaculates from mature boars (n=15) will be manually fractionated into P1, P2, and P3. Each fraction will be assessed for standard quality parameters (concentration, motility, viability via flow cytometry) and chromatin stability (SCSA). Processed doses from each fraction, standardized to 3 billion total sperm, will be used for single, fixed-time AI in a large-scale field trial (≥ 150 sows per fraction). Fertility will be evaluated via farrowing rate and total litter size. Statistical analysis will use generalized linear mixed models to compare outcomes between fractions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct, field-validated evidence on the fertility potential of non-P1 sperm fractions. It will establish practical protocols for their use, potentially increasing the number of AI doses obtained per ejaculate. The results will refine our understanding of sperm physiological competence beyond simple fraction origin.
proposals.novelty: While fraction characteristics are well-described, a direct, large-scale fertility comparison using only later fractions in a commercial setting has not been conclusively performed. This study explicitly tests the necessity of the P1 fraction, a core tenet of boar semen processing.

proposals.title: Discriminating the immunological roles of the post-mating uterine polymorphonuclear neutrophil influx in swine: a comparative assessment of sperm clearance versus pathogen defense
proposals.motivation: In pigs, a significant influx of polymorphonuclear neutrophils (PMNs) into the uterine lumen occurs after mating or insemination, a phenomenon historically interpreted as a mechanism for clearing excess or defective spermatozoa. However, this response coincides with the introduction of potential pathogens and seminal plasma immunomodulators. Clarifying the primary biological driver of this PMN influx is crucial for refining reproductive management strategies, as current practices may inadvertently trigger unnecessary inflammatory responses that could impair fertility.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary physiological function of the post-mating uterine PMN influx in swine is pathogen clearance and immune surveillance, rather than the active removal of viable, capacitation-competent spermatozoa.
proposals.method: We will use a controlled in vivo model comparing intrauterine challenges in sows: 1) extended boar semen prepared via NCSU-23 medium and purified by density gradient centrifugation (sperm-only), 2) a defined bacterial pathogen (e.g., E. coli), 3) semen spiked with the same pathogen, and 4) a control medium. Uterine lavages collected at 4-6 hours post-infusion will be analyzed via flow cytometry. A dual-parameter gating strategy will quantify the proportion of PMNs that are phagocytosing sperm (using sperm-specific chromatin staining with Hoechst 33342) versus bacteria (using pHrodo Green E. coli BioParticles). Sperm chromatin integrity and capacitation status (via chlortetracycline staining) in the non-phagocytosed population will also be assessed.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly quantify the targeting specificity of uterine PMNs, providing definitive evidence for their primary role in swine reproduction. It will challenge the paradigm of 'sperm cleansing' and inform the development of semen extenders and insemination protocols that minimize sterile inflammation while maintaining robust immune defense, potentially improving fertility outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While PMN influx is well-documented, no pre-2026 study has simultaneously and quantitatively compared sperm versus pathogen phagocytosis by uterine neutrophils in a physiologically relevant porcine model to discriminate their primary functional target.

proposals.title: Optimizing Artificial Insemination Doses in Boars: A Fraction-Specific Approach to Seminal Plasma Reconstitution for Enhanced Fertility
proposals.motivation: In swine AI, standard doses are prepared using sperm-rich fractions but are diluted in extenders that do not replicate the native seminal plasma (SP) environment. Pre-2026 research indicates the first ejaculate fraction (P1) has a distinct SP proteome and lipid composition crucial for sperm capacitation and oviduct interaction. This mismatch may impair sperm function post-thaw, contributing to variable fertility outcomes in commercial operations.
proposals.hypothesis: Reconstituting AI extenders with key biochemical components identified in the native P1 seminal plasma fraction will improve post-thaw sperm chromatin integrity, capacitation dynamics, and in vivo fertility compared to standard commercial extenders.
proposals.method: We will collect and biochemically profile (via HPLC and targeted mass spectrometry) the SP from the P1 fraction of high-fertility boars to identify characteristic proteins (e.g., PSP-I/II) and lipids. Using a defined base medium (e.g., Modena or NCSU-23), we will formulate experimental extenders by adding purified or recombinant P1-specific factors at physiological concentrations. Processed spermatozoa will be stored in these extenders, and assessed pre- and post-cryopreservation for motility (CASA), viability, chromatin stability (SCSA flow cytometry), and capacitation status (chlortetracycline assay). The optimal formulation will be tested in a fertility trial using a standardized porcine AI model.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for fraction-specific semen extenders, directly linking seminal plasma biochemistry to practical AI protocol optimization. It will provide a refined biological model for studying boar sperm capacitation and deliver a tangible strategy to reduce sperm dose numbers per AI while maintaining or improving farrowing rates and litter sizes.
proposals.novelty: While SP addition to extenders is studied, this proposal uniquely focuses on mimicking the specific composition of the physiologically relevant P1 fraction, rather than using whole SP or generic additives, to create a more natural microenvironment for sperm storage and function.

proposals.title: In Vivo Selection of P1 and P2 Sperm Subpopulations for Oviductal Reservoir Formation in the Boar
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 flow cytometric analyses have identified two distinct boar sperm subpopulations (P1 and P2) based on viability, mitochondrial activity, and chromatin status, with P1 sperm correlating with higher fertility. The oviductal sperm reservoir is a critical site for sperm selection and capacitation, yet it is unknown whether this in vivo selection process favors the P1 subpopulation. Directly testing this link is essential to understand the physiological relevance of these lab-identified subpopulations for reproductive success.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that P1 spermatozoa, characterized by high viability and stable chromatin, are preferentially bound and maintained within the oviductal sperm reservoir compared to P2 spermatozoa in vivo.
proposals.method: We will use a dual-label, in vivo approach. Fresh boar semen will be stained with Hoechst 33342 and MitoTracker Deep Red to identify P1 (high mitochondrial membrane potential, viable) and P2 (lower potential) subpopulations via flow cytometry (pre-sort analysis). Equal numbers of sorted P1 and P2 sperm will be fluorescently labeled with distinct, non-interfering cell membrane dyes (e.g., PKH26 vs. PKH67). These labeled, mixed sperm populations will be surgically inseminated into synchronized sows near ovulation. Oviducts will be recovered 8-12 hours post-insemination, and the isthmic reservoir will be flushed. The ratio of P1:P2 sperm recovered from the reservoir will be quantified using fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry, comparing it to the inseminated ratio.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct experimental evidence linking the functionally defined P1/P2 sperm subpopulations to a key in vivo selection event. It will validate the physiological significance of flow cytometric sperm assessments for fertility prediction. The findings could refine sperm processing and storage methods (e.g., NCSU medium-based protocols, centrifugation regimes) to better preserve the P1 subpopulation for artificial insemination.
proposals.novelty: While P1/P2 subpopulations are defined in vitro and correlated with fertility outcomes, their differential behavior in the critical oviductal reservoir has never been directly tested. This proposal moves beyond correlation to establish a causal link between a lab-identified sperm phenotype and a specific, fundamental in vivo reproductive function.

proposals.title: Spatial Mapping of Seminal Plasma Protein Microdomains on Boar Sperm Membranes Using MALDI-Mass Spectrometry Imaging: Implications for Capacitation and Fertility
proposals.motivation: The boar seminal plasma (SP) is a complex fluid critical for sperm function, modulating capacitation and fertility. However, pre-2026 knowledge is limited to bulk analyses, which obscure the precise spatial distribution and microdomain organization of SP components on the sperm surface. Understanding this spatial architecture is essential to decipher how specific SP proteins regulate sperm function during storage and transit in the female tract.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific SP proteins, including fertility-associated markers, form distinct spatial microdomains on the boar sperm membrane, and that these domains are dynamically altered by capacitation and storage conditions.
proposals.method: We will apply Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization Mass Spectrometry Imaging (MALDI-MSI) to map SP components directly on intact, individual boar spermatozoa. Sperm samples, prepared under defined conditions (fresh, after NCSU-23 medium incubation, post-centrifugation, and after in vitro capacitation), will be spotted onto conductive slides, coated with a suitable matrix (e.g., α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid), and analyzed using a MALDI-TOF/TOF instrument. Specific m/z values corresponding to known SP proteins (e.g., PSP-I/PSP-II, AQN, AWN families) will be targeted, and their spatial distribution will be correlated with functional assays (e.g., chlortetracycline staining for capacitation status).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first spatial proteomic atlas of SP components on boar sperm, directly linking molecular localization to physiological states. It will identify key protein microdomains involved in sperm resilience during storage and the capacitation process, offering new biomarkers for flow cytometric fertility assessments and improved semen extenders.
proposals.novelty: While MS analyzes SP composition and flow cytometry assesses sperm function, the direct, label-free spatial imaging of SP protein distribution on sperm membranes at a single-cell level using MALDI-MSI is a novel and untapped approach in andrology, moving beyond bulk biochemical averages.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Profiling Reveals Distinct Molecular Signatures in Boar Sperm Fractions P1 and P2: Implications for Sperm Maturation and Fertility
proposals.motivation: In boars, the ejaculate is fractionated into sperm-rich (P1) and post-rich (P2) portions, which exhibit documented differences in motility, morphology, and chromatin stability, impacting fertility and storage potential. However, the underlying molecular heterogeneity and functional states of spermatozoa within these fractions remain poorly characterized at the transcriptomic level. Applying single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) to these defined populations, a technique not yet widely used on mature sperm, can directly link ejaculate fraction to sperm molecular physiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that P1 and P2 sperm populations harbor distinct, non-random transcriptional profiles reflective of differential post-testicular maturation states, and that these molecular signatures correlate with known functional differences in capacitation potential and chromatin integrity.
proposals.method: Semen from multiple boars will be collected, and P1 and P2 fractions will be separated using standard protocols. Individual sperm cells will be isolated, and libraries will be prepared using a droplet-based scRNA-seq platform (e.g., 10x Genomics Chromium). Sequencing data will be processed using Cell Ranger, followed by downstream analysis in R with Seurat. We will perform differential gene expression analysis, pathway enrichment (using KEGG/GO databases), and pseudotime trajectory analysis (Monocle3) to infer developmental or functional state transitions between fractions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, single-cell transcriptomic atlas of boar sperm fractions, identifying key genes and pathways associated with superior sperm quality. It will establish a direct molecular link between ejaculate fraction and sperm function, offering novel biomarkers for predicting sperm resilience during storage and fertility outcomes in artificial insemination programs.
proposals.novelty: While flow cytometry and bulk assays have been used to study sperm populations, applying scRNA-seq to fractionated, mature boar sperm is a novel approach to deconvolute cellular heterogeneity within the ejaculate. This moves beyond correlative physical measurements to define causative molecular states governing sperm function post-ejaculation.

proposals.title: Quantitative Impact of Specific Sperm Morphological Defects on In Vitro Fertilization Success and Embryonic Development in Boars
proposals.motivation: Standard semen analysis for boars often treats all morphological abnormalities as equally detrimental to fertility, a simplification that may misguide breeding decisions and sperm processing. Given the economic importance of swine production and the widespread use of artificial insemination, a more nuanced understanding is needed. This study will directly test whether specific, well-defined defects (e.g., proximal vs. distal cytoplasmic droplets, bent vs. coiled tails, specific head abnormalities) differentially affect fertilization competence and early embryo quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific categories of sperm morphological defects, particularly those linked to functional deficiencies like chromatin compaction or motility apparatus integrity, will have a significantly greater negative impact on fertilization rates and subsequent embryonic development compared to other, more superficial abnormalities.
proposals.method: We will use extended boar semen from the NCSU facility, applying controlled centrifugation and density gradient (e.g., Percoll) protocols to isolate subpopulations enriched for specific, flow-cytometrically identified defects (e.g., using SYBR-14/PI for viability, Hoechst 33342 for morphology, and chromomycin A3 for chromatin status). These defined sperm populations will be used in standardized porcine in vitro fertilization (IVF) assays. Fertilization success will be assessed by pronucleus formation, and embryo development will be tracked to the blastocyst stage, with quality evaluated by blastocyst cell number and TUNEL assay for apoptosis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a functional ranking of sperm defects based on their actual biological impact, moving beyond descriptive morphology. It will deliver actionable criteria for developing more predictive semen quality tests for the swine industry. The findings will also advance fundamental knowledge of how specific structural anomalies compromise sperm function during capacitation and fertilization.
proposals.novelty: While sperm morphology is routinely assessed, this study uniquely combines precise defect isolation via advanced flow cytometry with functional IVF and embryo development endpoints to directly quantify the fertility penalty of specific abnormalities, a level of causal resolution not commonly achieved in livestock andrology.

proposals.title: Field Validation of Single Layer Centrifugation (SLC) in Boars: Correlating Sperm Morphology and Chromatin Integrity with On-Farm Fertility Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Single Layer Centrifugation (SLC) with Androcoll-P™ is a promising pre-2026 technique for selecting high-quality boar spermatozoa, improving motility and reducing chromatin damage in vitro. However, a critical gap exists between these in vitro quality improvements and documented, on-farm fertility results. Establishing a direct, field-based correlation is essential for commercial swine AI centers to justify adopting SLC as a routine processing method to enhance reproductive efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that boar sperm samples processed by SLC will demonstrate superior morphological integrity and chromatin stability compared to controls processed by standard centrifugation, and that these improvements will be directly correlated with increased farrowing rates and litter sizes in commercial artificial insemination (AI) programs.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, split-ejaculate field trial using semen from 30 boars at a commercial AI station. For each ejaculate, one aliquot will be processed via standard protocol (control), and the other via SLC with Androcoll-P™. Processed doses will be assessed for sperm morphology (computer-assisted sperm analysis, CASA) and chromatin integrity (flow cytometric Sperm Chromatin Structure Assay, SCSA). A minimum of 1500 sows will be inseminated in a blinded design with either SLC or control doses. Fertility outcomes (pregnancy rate at 30 days, farrowing rate, total born) will be recorded and statistically correlated with the in vitro sperm quality parameters using multivariate regression models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, field-validated evidence linking the specific sperm quality enhancements from SLC (morphology, chromatin) to tangible fertility outcomes in swine. It will deliver a predictive model for fertility based on post-processing sperm assays, offering AI centers a data-driven tool for quality control and process optimization.
proposals.novelty: While SLC's in vitro benefits are known, this proposal directly tests its causal link to commercial fertility, moving beyond laboratory correlations to practical validation. The integrated use of advanced flow cytometric chromatin analysis (SCSA) with large-scale field data in a split-sample design is a novel approach to bridge the gap between sperm processing technology and production outcomes.

proposals.title: Optimization of Single-Layer Centrifugation (SLC) using novel colloid compositions and centrifugation protocols for the selection of high-quality, capacitation-competent boar spermatozoa
proposals.motivation: Single-Layer Centrifugation (SLC) with Androcoll-P is a promising, practical method for boar sperm selection, but its protocol is largely standardized. Pre-2026 knowledge indicates that subtle variations in colloid composition (e.g., molecular weight, osmolarity) and centrifugation parameters (g-force, time) could differentially select sperm subpopulations based on motility, membrane integrity, and chromatin stability. A systematic optimization is needed to maximize the yield of sperm with superior fertility potential, particularly for artificial insemination and in vitro fertilization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically modifying the physicochemical properties of the colloid medium and the applied centrifugation force/time will selectively enrich a sperm population with enhanced motility, membrane stability, intact chromatin, and in vitro capacitation ability, surpassing the performance of the standard Androcoll-P protocol.
proposals.method: We will use fresh boar ejaculates and perform a factorial design experiment. Colloid variables: type (Androcoll-P vs. custom silica colloids with varying particle sizes), concentration (70-90%), and osmolarity (290-320 mOsm/kg). Centrifugation variables: g-force (300-800 x g) and time (15-30 min). Post-selection, sperm quality will be assessed via CASA for motility, flow cytometry for membrane integrity (SYBR-14/PI), chromatin status (SCSA or acridine orange), and capacitation status (chlortetracycline fluorescence assay). Fertility potential will be evaluated using an in vitro sperm-oocyte binding assay.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorously optimized, evidence-based SLC protocol for boar sperm, directly linking specific colloid and centrifugation parameters to measurable sperm quality and function markers. It will establish a refined tool for semen processing labs to improve the consistency and fertility of doses for artificial insemination, potentially increasing reproductive efficiency in swine production.
proposals.novelty: While SLC is established, a systematic, multi-factorial optimization of both colloid composition and centrifugation kinetics specifically for boar sperm, with concurrent evaluation of chromatin integrity and capacitation competence, has not been comprehensively reported in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Single Layer Centrifugation with Porcine Colloid Formulations Selects for High-Quality Spermatozoa and Reduces Seminal Viral Load in Boar Semen
proposals.motivation: In swine artificial insemination, semen processing aims to select fertile sperm and ensure biosafety by removing pathogens like Porcine Reproductive and Respiratory Syndrome Virus (PRRSV) and Porcine Circovirus 2 (PCV2). Pre-2026, Single Layer Centrifugation (SLC) using colloids like Androcoll-P is established for selecting robust, non-capacitated boar sperm based on motility and membrane integrity. However, its potential as a concurrent, non-antibiotic-dependent method for reducing specific viral contaminants in seminal plasma remains underexplored, representing a critical gap for improving herd health and fertility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimizing the density and viscosity parameters of a porcine-specific colloid for SLC will concurrently select a sperm population with superior chromatin integrity and capacitation potential while effectively partitioning and removing seminal pathogens based on differential sedimentation and surface charge interactions.
proposals.method: Fresh boar ejaculates will be spiked with known titers of PRRSV and PCV2. We will test modified formulations of the commercial colloid Androcoll-P (Sigma) by adjusting density gradients (e.g., 70% vs. 90% colloidal silica) and incorporating surface-modifying agents like hyaluronic acid. Sperm quality post-SLC will be assessed via CASA for kinematics, flow cytometry for membrane integrity (SYBR-14/PI) and chromatin status (acridine orange), and chlortetracycline assay for capacitation. Pathogen removal efficacy will be quantified using qPCR on the seminal plasma fraction and the sperm pellet to measure viral copy number reduction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, single-step protocol for boar semen processing that enhances sperm quality for storage and fertility while mitigating viral transmission risk. It will establish specific colloid physicochemical properties required for dual selection and pathogen removal, offering a practical alternative or supplement to antibiotic use in semen extenders.
proposals.novelty: While SLC is known for sperm selection, its deliberate design and validation as a concurrent physical barrier for specific swine viruses in semen is novel. This moves beyond general 'debris removal' to target documented, economically critical pathogens using a method aligned with Rodriguez-Martinez's expertise in boar sperm physiology and capacitation.

proposals.title: Defining a Beneficial Pre-IVF Storage Window in Seminal Plasma: Chromatin Stability and Capacitation Dynamics in Boar Spermatozoa
proposals.motivation: Pre-IVF sperm handling protocols often assume minimal contact with seminal plasma (SP) to avoid premature capacitation and oxidative damage, potentially discarding beneficial effects. In pigs, SP contains key factors for sperm survival and function. This study challenges the dogma that shorter storage is always better by systematically testing if a defined SP exposure period enhances sperm chromatin integrity and synchronizes capacitation, thereby improving IVF outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: A specific, short-term storage of boar spermatozoa in their native seminal plasma prior to IVF processing will enhance chromatin stability and promote a more uniform, inducible capacitation response, leading to increased fertilization rates and embryo quality compared to immediate processing or prolonged storage.
proposals.method: Fresh boar ejaculates will be aliquoted and stored in SP at 17°C for 0h (immediate processing), 1h, 2h, 4h, and 8h. Post-storage, sperm will be washed and prepared for IVF using standard NCSU-based media. Sperm quality will be assessed pre- and post-storage via: 1) Flow cytometric evaluation of chromatin status (SCSA: DNA Fragmentation Index) and membrane lipid disorder (M540/Yo-Pro-1 staining for capacitation state), 2) Computer-assisted sperm analysis (CASA) for motility, and 3) Assessment of in vitro fertilization outcomes (cleavage and blastocyst rates). Statistical modeling will identify optimal time windows.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a refined, evidence-based protocol for pre-IVF boar sperm storage, potentially increasing the efficiency of porcine assisted reproduction. It will elucidate the temporal relationship between SP exposure, chromatin compaction, and capacitation readiness, contributing fundamental knowledge on sperm physiology.
proposals.novelty: The study moves beyond the simplistic 'SP is detrimental' view to identify a potential beneficial temporal niche, integrating chromatin assessment (Rodriguez-Martinez's expertise) with capacitation kinetics to define a holistic storage window, a nuanced approach not previously explored in porcine IVF.

proposals.title: Real-time visualization of hyaluronic acid-mediated sperm rheotaxis and hyperactivation during porcine in vitro fertilization
proposals.motivation: Hyaluronic acid (HA) is a key component of the oviductal and cumulus matrix, known to select for sperm with high DNA integrity and to influence capacitation. However, the precise, real-time behavioral dynamics of boar sperm interacting with HA gradients near the oocyte remain poorly characterized, limiting our understanding of this critical pre-fertilization checkpoint. This gap hinders the optimization of sperm selection media for porcine assisted reproductive technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that HA establishes a chemokinetic and rheotactic gradient that selectively promotes the sustained hyperactivation and directed motility of chromatin-intact boar spermatozoa, facilitating their penetration of the cumulus-oocyte complex.
proposals.method: We will use high-resolution differential interference contrast (DIC) and fluorescence time-lapse microscopy on a microfluidic platform generating stable HA gradients. Fresh boar semen, processed using established NCSU-23 medium and centrifugation protocols, will be labeled with Hoechst 33342 and MitoTracker Deep Red to concurrently assess chromatin status (via post-hoc flow cytometry) and midpiece activity. Sperm trajectories will be tracked and analyzed using the open-source TrackMate plugin for Fiji/ImageJ, quantifying parameters like curvilinear velocity, lateral head displacement, and chemotactic index.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, quantitative spatiotemporal map of HA-sperm interactions in pigs, linking specific motility patterns to sperm chromatin quality. It will establish objective, imaging-based biomarkers for predicting sperm fertilizing capacity, directly informing the design of advanced sperm storage and capacitation media to improve swine IVF and semen preservation.
proposals.novelty: While HA-binding assays are common, directly visualizing and quantifying the dynamic behavioral response of individual, chromatin-classified boar sperm to HA in a controlled gradient is novel. The integration of live-cell imaging with microfluidics and post-hoc flow cytometric chromatin analysis on the same sperm population represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Quantification and Functional Significance of Hyaluronic Acid Receptor Density on Boar Spermatozoa: A Biochemical Binding Assay Approach
proposals.motivation: Hyaluronic acid (HA) binding is a recognized biomarker for sperm maturity and fertility in several species, including pigs. However, pre-2026 research on porcine sperm relies primarily on qualitative microscopic HA-binding assays, lacking precise quantification of receptor presence. A quantitative biochemical method is needed to establish robust correlations between HA receptor density, sperm quality parameters (e.g., chromatin integrity, capacitation status), and fertility outcomes in boars.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the density of functional HA receptors on the boar sperm plasma membrane, quantified via a standardized biochemical binding assay, is positively correlated with sperm chromatin stability and in vitro capacitation success, serving as a superior predictor of fertility compared to traditional semen parameters.
proposals.method: We will adapt a solid-phase biochemical binding assay from cell adhesion studies. Washed spermatozoa from fresh boar ejaculates will be incubated in microtiter plates coated with defined concentrations of biotinylated HA. Bound sperm will be quantified using a colorimetric assay for sperm-specific enzymes (e.g., hexosaminidase). Parallel samples will be assessed for chromatin status (SCSA/Sperm-Halomax) and capacitation (CTC staining, tyrosine phosphorylation via western blot). Binding kinetics (saturation, specificity) will be determined, and receptor density will be correlated with functional outcomes using multivariate regression analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first standardized, quantitative assay for HA receptor density on boar sperm, moving beyond binary binding assessments. It will establish a direct, measurable link between a molecular sperm surface marker and key fertility-related cellular states (chromatin compaction, capacitation readiness), offering a novel, objective parameter for boar semen evaluation and storage protocol optimization.
proposals.novelty: While HA binding tests exist, applying quantitative biochemical binding kinetics from cell biology to porcine sperm is novel.
