This approach allows for the precise measurement of receptor abundance and avidity, which has not been done in the context of boar sperm capacitation and chromatin quality, integrating Dr. Rodriguez-Martinez's core research themes.

proposals.title: Economic and Practical Evaluation of Hyaluronic Acid Supplementation for Scaling Up Commercial Porcine In Vitro Fertilization
proposals.motivation: Porcine IVF remains less efficient and more costly than in vivo breeding, limiting its widespread commercial adoption for genetic improvement. Hyaluronic acid (HA) is known to improve sperm selection and capacitation in vitro, but its use has been confined to small-scale, research-grade protocols. A systematic assessment of HA's cost-benefit ratio and integration into large-scale, standardized IVF workflows is critically needed to determine its commercial viability.
proposals.hypothesis: Supplementing porcine IVF medium with a defined concentration of HA will significantly improve fertilization and blastocyst rates in a high-throughput system without proportionally increasing operational costs, thereby enhancing the overall economic feasibility of commercial pig IVF.
proposals.method: We will use commercial boar ejaculates, processed via standard NCSU-23 medium and centrifugation protocols, to establish high-volume IVF groups (≥1000 oocytes per trial). Sperm will be co-incubated with in vitro-matured oocytes in media supplemented with 0 (control), 0.75, or 1.5 mg/mL HA (pre-2026 effective doses). Key endpoints will be fertilization rate (pronuclei formation), blastocyst yield, and cell number. A detailed cost analysis will track all reagent, labor, and facility expenses per transferable embryo produced across groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive dataset linking a biochemical sperm capacitation aid (HA) to direct production costs in porcine IVF. It will deliver a validated, scalable protocol and a clear decision framework for producers considering HA adoption, bridging a critical gap between basic sperm physiology research and applied reproductive technology.
proposals.novelty: While HA's biological effects on sperm are known, its evaluation within a rigorous economic and high-throughput production context, specifically for commercial pig IVF, is novel and addresses a primary industry barrier to implementation.

proposals.title: Hyaluronan-mediated modulation of boar sperm chromatin stability and capacitation kinetics in an oviductal fluid model
proposals.motivation: The oviductal microenvironment is crucial for sustaining a viable sperm reservoir and regulating capacitation for successful fertilization. Hyaluronan (HA) is a major glycosaminoglycan in oviductal fluid, but its precise mechanistic role in porcine sperm physiology remains poorly defined. Understanding how HA interacts with sperm to maintain viability and delay capacitation is key to improving in vitro fertilization protocols and developing advanced sperm storage media for swine reproduction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hyaluronan in the oviductal fluid interacts with the boar sperm surface to stabilize chromatin structure and suppress premature capacitation by modulating intracellular calcium flux and protein tyrosine phosphorylation patterns.
proposals.method: We will use a defined in vitro oviductal fluid medium, incorporating purified HA at physiological concentrations, to incubate boar spermatozoa selected via a standardized Percoll gradient centrifugation protocol. Sperm viability, capacitation status (via chlortetracycline staining and flow cytometric assessment of tyrosine phosphorylation), and chromatin stability (using the sperm chromatin structure assay, SCSA) will be evaluated at timed intervals. Specific comparisons will be made between HA-supplemented media, control media, and media containing HAase to disrupt HA function.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate the specific biochemical pathways through which HA influences boar sperm function, linking extracellular matrix signaling to intracellular capacitation events and nuclear integrity. It will provide a mechanistic foundation for refining sperm handling and storage media, such as NCSU-23 variants, to better mimic the oviductal environment and enhance fertility outcomes in artificial insemination.
proposals.novelty: While HA's presence is known, this study uniquely integrates the analysis of its effects on both the functional process of capacitation and the structural parameter of chromatin stability in boar sperm, using a physiologically relevant model to dissect its specific regulatory role.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptional Profiling of Boar Spermatozoa from the Oviduct Reveals Region-Specific Functional States Underlying Capacitation
proposals.motivation: Sperm capacitation is a crucial, final maturation step required for fertilization, predominantly occurring within the oviduct. However, pre-2026 knowledge of this process is largely based on in vitro studies or bulk analyses, masking the heterogeneity and precise transcriptional adaptations of individual spermatozoa in their native physiological environment. Mapping the transcriptional and functional states of sperm within specific oviductal regions is essential to understand the molecular basis of sperm selection and acquisition of fertilizing competence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spermatozoa residing in different anatomical regions of the porcine oviduct (isthmus, ampulla) exhibit distinct, region-specific transcriptional profiles that correlate with discrete functional states of capacitation and readiness for fertilization.
proposals.method: We will collect spermatozoa from the isthmic and ampullary regions of sow oviducts post-mating. Individual sperm cells will be processed using a commercially available, low-input single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) platform (e.g., 10x Genomics Chromium). Bioinformatic analysis will involve standard pre-processing (Cell Ranger), dimensionality reduction (Seurat, UMAP), and unsupervised clustering to identify transcriptional subpopulations. Differential expression analysis between regional clusters will be performed using established algorithms (e.g., MAST, Wilcoxon rank-sum test) to define region-specific gene signatures, which will be functionally annotated via Gene Ontology (GO) and KEGG pathway enrichment.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, single-cell transcriptomic atlas of spermatozoa within the physiological context of the porcine oviduct. It will directly link oviductal microenvironment to sperm molecular states, identifying novel biomarkers for advanced capacitation and fertility. The findings will refine in vitro capacitation media and storage protocols by providing targeted molecular benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While scRNA-seq has been applied to testicular cells, its application to mature, post-ejaculatory spermatozoa retrieved directly from the oviductal lumen is novel. This approach moves beyond inferring capacitation states from bulk assays or in vitro models to directly profiling the authentic, heterogeneous sperm population at the site of fertilization.

proposals.title: Metabolic Flux Analysis of Boar Spermatozoa Reveals Distinct Bioenergetic Profiles in the Oviductal Reservoir versus the Ampullary-Isthmic Junction
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Rodriguez-Martinez's work, establishes the oviduct as a key modulator of sperm capacitation and fertility. However, the specific metabolic adaptations sperm undergo as they transition from the storage reservoir to the fertilization site remain poorly characterized. Defining these energy utilization pathways is critical for understanding the physiological triggers of capacitation and for improving in vitro fertilization and sperm storage media in swine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spermatozoa residing in the oviductal reservoir utilize a predominantly glycolytic metabolism to maintain viability in a quiescent state, while spermatozoa in the ampullary-isthmic junction shift towards oxidative phosphorylation to meet the high energy demands of hyperactivation and capacitation.
proposals.method: We will collect sperm from the uterotubal junction (reservoir) and ampullary-isthmic junction of pre-ovulatory sows. Using targeted metabolomics via LC-MS/MS, we will quantify extracellular flux of key metabolites (glucose, lactate, pyruvate, oxygen). Intracellular metabolic flux analysis will be performed by applying constraint-based modeling (e.g., using the COBRA Toolbox in MATLAB) to a boar sperm-specific metabolic network reconstruction, integrating our flux data and pre-2026 genomic annotations to predict pathway activities (glycolysis, TCA cycle, oxidative phosphorylation).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative map of metabolic flux in sperm within distinct physiological compartments of the porcine oviduct. It will directly link spatial localization to bioenergetic state, offering mechanistic insight into how the oviductal microenvironment sequentially regulates sperm function. The findings will inform the design of advanced capacitation and storage media that mimic these natural metabolic transitions.
proposals.novelty: While metabolic profiling of ejaculated or in vitro-capacitated sperm exists, applying constraint-based metabolic flux analysis to sperm directly recovered from defined anatomical regions of the oviduct is a novel approach. This moves beyond static metabolite snapshots to a dynamic, systems-level understanding of energy metabolism in situ.

proposals.title: Real-time intravital imaging of boar spermatozoa dynamics in the oviductal isthmus: linking motility patterns to capacitation status
proposals.motivation: Despite extensive in vitro studies on boar sperm capacitation, the precise spatiotemporal dynamics of sperm within the oviduct remain poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to link sperm storage, selection, and functional changes to fertility outcomes in swine reproduction. Advanced intravital microscopy now offers a path to directly observe these critical events in a physiologically relevant context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, quantifiable motility patterns and spatial distributions of boar spermatozoa within the oviductal isthmus are directly correlated with specific stages of chromatin remodeling and capacitation.
proposals.method: We will adapt a dorsal skinfold chamber model in anesthetized sows, combined with high-resolution two-photon intravital microscopy, to image sperm labeled with the chromatin-sensitive fluorophore Hoechst 33342 and the membrane potential-sensitive dye JC-1. Sperm motility and location will be tracked in real time using the TrackMate plugin for Fiji/ImageJ, employing the Linear Assignment Problem (LAP) tracker algorithm to handle high-density sperm populations. Concurrently, sperm will be recovered via micro-catheter for flow cytometric analysis of capacitation markers (e.g., tyrosine phosphorylation, membrane fluidity) and chromatin integrity to correlate imaging data with functional status.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, dynamic map of boar sperm behavior in the oviduct, establishing a causal link between in vivo motility, location, and molecular capacitation events. It will yield novel biomarkers for predicting sperm fertilizing potential and inform improved semen handling and storage protocols by revealing the physiological requirements for successful oviductal transit.
proposals.novelty: While intravital imaging has been used in mice, its application to the porcine reproductive tract, combined with the concurrent molecular analysis of sperm recovered from the exact imaging site, is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond static endpoint assays to capture the dynamic process of sperm selection and maturation in a large-animal model of high agricultural importance.

proposals.title: Development of a High-Throughput Flow Cytometric Platform to Screen Oviductal Fluid Components for Capacitation-Modulating Effects in Boar Spermatozoa
proposals.motivation: Boar sperm capacitation is a complex, incompletely understood process critical for fertilization, known to be influenced by oviductal fluid (OF) components. Current methods for studying capacitation are low-throughput and labor-intensive, limiting systematic screening of the numerous proteins, metabolites, and extracellular vesicles present in OF. A high-throughput screening (HTS) platform is needed to efficiently identify key capacitation modulators, which could revolutionize semen extenders and fertility diagnostics in swine reproduction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating automated liquid handling with multi-parameter flow cytometry will enable the rapid, quantitative screening of OF fractions, identifying specific compounds that either promote or inhibit key capacitation events like cholesterol efflux, protein tyrosine phosphorylation, and calcium flux.
proposals.method: We will develop an automated platform using a liquid handling robot to prepare 96-well plates containing boar spermatozoa (processed via established NCSU-based centrifugation and medium protocols) incubated with individual OF fractions or synthetic compounds. Capacitation status will be assessed in each well using a panel of fluorescent probes (e.g., M540 for membrane disorder, Fluo-4 for calcium, anti-phosphotyrosine antibodies) analyzed by a high-throughput flow cytometer (e.g., IntelliCyt iQue or equivalent). Data analysis will employ automated gating algorithms and machine learning (e.g., Random Forest classification) to correlate compound presence with specific capacitation-related phenotypic signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first automated, quantitative platform for high-throughput capacitation screening in livestock sperm, generating a functional map of OF components. It will identify novel capacitation agonists and antagonists, providing direct candidates for improving semen storage media and diagnostic biomarkers for sperm fertility potential, bridging a critical gap between basic capacitation research and clinical application.
proposals.novelty: While flow cytometry is used for sperm analysis, its integration with full automation and machine learning for systematic screening of native oviductal compounds on capacitation is novel. This moves beyond descriptive OF characterization to high-throughput functional analysis, a significant methodological advance over existing low-throughput assays.

proposals.title: Molecular Dynamics Simulation of Boar Sperm Protein Interactions with Oviductal Glycans During Capacitation
proposals.motivation: The oviduct is critical for sperm capacitation and selection, but the specific molecular interactions between sperm surface proteins (e.g., AWN, SPADH1) and oviductal fluid components (e.g., glycosaminoglycans, glycoproteins) remain poorly characterized. A detailed mechanistic understanding is essential for improving in vitro capacitation media and sperm storage protocols, directly impacting swine fertility and artificial insemination outcomes. Current knowledge, including Rodriguez-Martinez's work on the physiological roles of the boar ejaculate and oviduct involvement, lacks this molecular-resolution perspective.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, quantifiable binding affinities and conformational changes between boar sperm surface proteins and oviductal glycan motifs are critical for initiating capacitation and forming the oviductal sperm reservoir.
proposals.method: We will construct all-atom and coarse-grained molecular dynamics (MD) simulation systems using GROMACS. Protein structures will be sourced from homology modeling (SWISS-MODEL) based on known boar sperm protein sequences, and glycan structures from the GlyTouCan database. Simulations will employ the CHARMM36 force field with specific carbohydrate parameters. Binding free energies will be calculated using the Molecular Mechanics Poisson-Boltzmann Surface Area (MM-PBSA) method, comparing proteins in pre-capacitation vs. capacitation-like states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first atomistic-scale model of key sperm-oviduct interactions in pigs, identifying specific molecular targets for fertility biomarkers. It will generate testable predictions for in vitro experiments, guiding the rational design of capacitation media and sperm storage extenders that mimic oviductal conditions.
proposals.novelty: While flow cytometry and biochemical assays have described sperm capacitation, applying high-resolution MD simulations to model the dynamic interaction of boar-specific proteins with the oviductal glycome is a novel, hypothesis-generating approach that moves beyond descriptive phenomenology.

proposals.title: Correlation of In Vitro Sperm Chromatin Resilience During Liquid Storage with In Vivo Fertility Outcomes in Boars
proposals.motivation: In pig AI, semen is stored in liquid form (e.g., NCSU-23 medium) for days, during which sperm quality declines. While lab assays (motility, viability) are used, they poorly predict field fertility. The integrity of sperm chromatin, crucial for successful fertilization and embryo development, is known to be sensitive to storage stress, but its dynamic degradation and direct link to in vivo outcomes are unclear.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the resilience of boar sperm chromatin to in vitro liquid storage stress, quantified by its degradation kinetics, is a superior predictor of in vivo fertility post-insemination compared to standard semen quality parameters measured at a single time point.
proposals.method: Semen from 30 boars of known fertility will be extended in NCSU-23 medium and stored at 17°C. Samples will be analyzed at 0, 24, 48, and 72h of storage. Chromatin status will be assessed using the Sperm Chromatin Structure Assay (SCSA) to measure DNA Fragmentation Index (DFI%) and High DNA Stainability (HDS). Kinetics of DFI increase will be modeled using linear and non-linear regression to derive a 'chromatin resilience score'. These scores, along with standard metrics (CASA motility, flow cytometric viability, acrosome status), will be correlated with farrowing rate and litter size from a minimum of 50 inseminations per boar using multivariable regression analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantitative link between a dynamic lab-based measure of sperm stress resilience and commercial fertility outcomes. It will validate chromatin degradation kinetics as a novel, functional biomarker for boar semen storage quality, moving beyond static snapshots. The results could refine semen processing protocols and boar selection for AI centers, improving reproductive efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While SCSA is used, its application as a kinetic measure of resilience during standard liquid storage to predict fertility is novel. This bridges the gap between a known physiological role (chromatin integrity) and a practical husbandry challenge (storage duration), testing if the *process* of decline is more informative than the *state* at collection.

proposals.title: Cross-species validation of the seminal plasma Oxidative Stress Index (OSI) as a predictive biomarker for fertility in artificial insemination programs
proposals.motivation: The Oxidative Stress Index (OSI), calculated as the ratio of reactive oxygen species to antioxidant capacity in seminal plasma, has shown promise in boars as a predictor of sperm quality and fertility outcomes for artificial insemination (AI). However, its generalizability across species remains untested. Validating this biomarker in other key livestock species and humans would provide a standardized, non-invasive tool to improve semen processing, storage, and selection for AI, directly addressing global needs for efficient animal production and assisted reproduction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the seminal plasma OSI is a conserved biomarker of fertility, and that a high OSI will correlate with reduced sperm chromatin integrity, impaired in vitro capacitation potential, and lower field fertility rates in bulls, stallions, and human donors.
proposals.method: We will collect fresh ejaculates from bulls, stallions, boars (positive control), and human donors (n=50/species). Seminal plasma will be separated via centrifugation. OSI will be determined using chemiluminescence assays for ROS and colorimetric kits for Total Antioxidant Capacity. Concurrently, spermatozoa will be assessed for chromatin stability (SCSA/SDF via flow cytometry), capacitation status (chlortetracycline assay), and motility (CASA). Fertility outcomes (field conception rates for livestock, or clinical pregnancy rates per donor for human samples used in IVF/ICSI) will be obtained retrospectively or prospectively. Statistical analysis will employ generalized linear models to correlate OSI with laboratory and fertility parameters across species.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the cross-species applicability of OSI, moving it from a species-specific observation to a potential universal biomarker. It will provide a practical, flow cytometry-compatible protocol for fertility prognosis, directly informing semen processing and storage guidelines in livestock AI centers and human sperm banks to enhance reproductive efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While OSI is studied within species, a deliberate, comparative multi-species validation linking OSI to functional sperm parameters (chromatin, capacitation) and field fertility is novel. This bridges the gap between basic oxidative stress research and applied andrology across mammalian reproduction.

proposals.title: Interaction of the sow oviductal fluid proteome with seminal oxidative stress index to modulate boar sperm capacitation and chromatin stability
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research establishes that the oxidative stress index (OSI) of boar semen is a critical determinant of sperm quality and fertility, but its impact is typically assessed in vitro. The sow's oviduct provides a complex microenvironment that can mitigate or exacerbate oxidative damage during sperm transport and capacitation. Understanding this interaction is essential for developing advanced semen extenders and diagnostic tools that account for the female's physiological contribution to fertility outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific proteins within the peri-ovulatory sow oviductal fluid will interact with semen of varying OSI levels, differentially influencing the success of sperm capacitation and the stability of sperm chromatin structure.
proposals.method: We will collect oviductal fluid from sows at the peri-ovulatory stage. Boar ejaculates will be characterized for OSI using flow cytometry (CellROX Green for ROS and BODIPY C11 for lipid peroxidation) and divided into high, medium, and low OSI groups. Sperm from each group will be co-incubated with oviductal fluid in a defined capacitation medium (based on NCSU-23). Outcomes will be assessed via: 1) Computer-Assisted Sperm Analysis for kinematic parameters, 2) chlortetracycline fluorescence assay for capacitation status, and 3) sperm chromatin structure assay (SCSA) using flow cytometric analysis of acridine orange staining to measure DNA fragmentation index (DFI).
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link a key male factor (seminal OSI) with specific female reproductive tract factors, moving beyond isolated semen analysis. It will identify oviductal fluid components that are protective or detrimental to sperm with high oxidative load, providing a mechanistic basis for subfertility. The findings will inform the development of targeted antioxidant therapies and refined semen handling protocols that consider the sperm-oviduct interaction.
proposals.novelty: While OSI and oviductal effects on sperm are studied separately, their dynamic interaction is poorly characterized. This proposal uniquely integrates precise OSI quantification with functional oviductal fluid exposure to assess combined effects on two critical fertility markers: capacitation and chromatin integrity, which is a novel, systems-level approach in porcine reproduction.

proposals.title: Systematic Evaluation of Individual Boar Variation Masked by Semen Pooling: Implications for Capacitation Assay Validation and Fertility Prediction
proposals.motivation: In porcine reproduction, pooled ejaculates are commonly used to validate sperm function assays, such as those for chromatin integrity and capacitation status, under the assumption they represent the general population. However, this practice may obscure significant inter-male variation in sperm quality and resilience, which is critical for accurate fertility prediction and personalized AI strategies. Given the economic importance of boar fertility and the physiological complexity of capacitation, it is essential to test this foundational assumption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pooling ejaculates from multiple boars for the validation of sperm functional assays (e.g., flow cytometric assessments of chromatin stability and capacitation) will statistically mask biologically significant individual male variation in sperm parameters, leading to reduced predictive power for individual fertility outcomes.
proposals.method: Semen will be collected from 30 mature, fertile boars and processed individually and in randomized pools (n=10 pools of 3 boars each). For each sample (individual and pool), we will perform standardized assessments pre- and post-in vitro capacitation using pre-2026 methods: computer-assisted sperm analysis (CASA) for motility, flow cytometry with fluorescent probes (e.g., Yo-Pro-1, M540, anti-phosphotyrosine antibodies) for viability, membrane fluidity, and capacitation status, and chromatin dispersion tests (SCD/Halo assay). Statistical analysis will compare variance components between individual and pooled samples using mixed models and assess the correlation of assay results with historical farrowing rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct, quantitative evidence on whether semen pooling is a valid strategy for assay development in boars. It will clarify the extent of individual variation in key functional traits post-capacitation, informing more robust experimental designs for fertility research. The findings will guide andrologists and breeding companies in optimizing semen evaluation protocols for precise male fertility prognosis.
proposals.novelty: While pooling is a widespread practice, its specific effect on masking the variance of advanced, flow cytometric sperm function parameters relevant to capacitation in boars has not been systematically tested. This study directly challenges a methodological norm in the field with direct implications for the interpretation of validation studies.

proposals.title: Quantitative Assessment of Canine Sperm Capacitation States Using Ratiometric Calcium Imaging and Flow Cytometry
proposals.motivation: Accurate assessment of sperm capacitation is critical for improving assisted reproductive technologies (ART) in dogs, a species with variable semen quality and fertility. Current canine sperm evaluation relies on indirect, endpoint assays (e.g., CTC staining) that lack the dynamic, quantitative resolution needed to characterize the capacitation-associated calcium signaling cascade. Adapting established fluorescent calcium indicators from robust mouse models offers a direct, physiologically relevant method to address this gap in canine andrology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the application of ratiometric calcium dyes (e.g., Fura-2 AM) will enable the quantification of distinct intracellular calcium ([Ca2+]i) signatures in dog sperm, and that these signatures will correlate with established markers of capacitation (e.g., protein tyrosine phosphorylation patterns and membrane fluidity changes).
proposals.method: We will collect and process dog semen using standard centrifugation and NCSU-based medium protocols. Sperm will be loaded with the ratiometric dye Fura-2 AM and incubated under non-capacitating and capacitating conditions. Using a fluorescence microscope equipped for ratiometric imaging, we will quantify baseline and stimulated [Ca2+]i dynamics in individual sperm. Parallel samples will be analyzed via flow cytometry to correlate calcium levels with population-wide capacitation status using the fluorescent probe M540 and immunodetection of tyrosine phosphorylation. Statistical analysis will compare calcium signatures across physiological states.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, quantitative framework for assessing canine sperm function based on direct measurement of a key physiological parameter ([Ca2+]i). It will provide the first detailed characterization of calcium dynamics during dog sperm capacitation, linking biophysical events to molecular markers. The validated protocol will be a significant tool for canine semen analysis, with potential to improve semen storage, fertility prediction, and ART outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While ratiometric calcium imaging is a gold standard in mouse sperm research, its application to quantify capacitation states in dog sperm is novel. This approach moves beyond static, population-averaged assays to provide dynamic, single-cell physiological data, a significant advancement over existing canine andrology methods.

proposals.title: Swim-up selection bias in boar sperm: A multi-parametric flow cytometric and functional analysis of excluded subpopulations
proposals.motivation: The swim-up technique is a standard method for selecting motile, morphologically normal sperm for assisted reproduction, predicated on the assumption that it isolates the most fertile cells. However, pre-2026 studies in pigs indicate that in vivo fertilization involves complex sperm-oviduct interactions and heterogeneous sperm populations. A critical, unanswered question is whether swim-up's passive migration criterion inadvertently excludes functionally competent sperm subpopulations that are crucial for natural fertilization, potentially explaining suboptimal outcomes in some assisted reproductive technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the conventional swim-up procedure selectively enriches for a specific subset of boar spermatozoa based on motility and density, while excluding other subpopulations that possess distinct chromatin integrity, capacitation potential, and oviduct-binding capabilities essential for successful in vivo fertilization.
proposals.method: We will use fresh boar ejaculates (n=15) and split each sample to compare pre-swim-up whole semen, swim-up selected fraction, and the swim-up excluded pellet. Multi-parametric flow cytometry (pre-2026 standard panels) will assess viability (SYBR-14/PI), acrosomal status (PNA-FITC), mitochondrial membrane potential (JC-1), chromatin stability (acridine orange test), and oxidative stress (CellROX). Functional assays will include in vitro capacitation kinetics (chlortetracycline staining) and binding to oviductal epithelial cell explants. Statistical analysis will employ principal component analysis (PCA) and K-means clustering to define subpopulations and compare their prevalence across fractions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, functional characterization of boar sperm subpopulations excluded by swim-up, directly linking selection bias to specific physiological markers. It will challenge the paradigm that swim-up universally selects the 'best' sperm for fertility, offering evidence-based rationale for refining or complementing current sperm preparation methods in porcine reproduction and potentially other species.
proposals.novelty: While swim-up is known to select for motility, its systematic bias against specific functional subpopulations defined by chromatin status and oviduct-binding affinity—key factors in porcine in vivo fertilization—remains unexplored. This integrates Rodriguez-Martinez's expertise in boar sperm physiology and oviductal interaction with advanced, pre-2026 flow cytometric subpopulation analysis.

proposals.title: Prospective validation of a multivariate sperm quality index for predicting field fertility in commercial boars
proposals.motivation: Despite extensive research on individual sperm traits (e.g., motility, morphology, chromatin integrity), their predictive power for field fertility in swine remains inconsistent. This gap hinders the development of reliable, laboratory-based fertility assays for boar semen used in artificial insemination, leading to economic inefficiencies in pork production. A prospective validation of combined trait models is a critical, missing step to translate laboratory findings into practical tools for the swine industry.
proposals.hypothesis: A multivariate model combining flow cytometric assessments of sperm viability, acrosomal status, chromatin stability, and capacitation status will prospectively predict farrowing rate and litter size in a new cohort of commercial boars with greater accuracy than any single traditional semen parameter.
proposals.method: Semen from 150 boars (new cohort) from a commercial nucleus will be analyzed using standardized protocols for sperm concentration, motility, and morphology. Flow cytometry will quantify combined traits: membrane integrity (SYBR-14/PI), acrosome status (PNA-FITC), chromatin stability (SCSA: %DFI, %HDS), and capacitation-like changes (chlortetracycline staining). A pre-existing multivariate model (e.g., logistic regression or random forest algorithm developed from prior retrospective data) will be applied to these results. These model scores will be correlated prospectively with field fertility data (farrowing rate, total born) from at least 50 inseminations per boar.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective evidence for a robust, laboratory-based fertility index for boar semen, directly linking a composite of sperm functional traits to reproductive outcomes. It will establish a validated protocol for integrating flow cytometric sperm analyses into boar fertility assessment, offering a tool for genetic selection and semen dose optimization to improve herd productivity.
proposals.novelty: While retrospective models exist, this work prospectively tests a combined-trait model in a new, independent cohort under field conditions, moving beyond correlation to true prediction. It uniquely integrates pre-capacitation status with chromatin and acrosomal metrics into a single predictive index for commercial swine.

proposals.title: Proteomic and Chromatin Integrity Signatures of High-Motility Boar Spermatozoa Isolated by Swim-Up: Correlates with In Vitro Capacitation Potential
proposals.motivation: The swim-up procedure is a standard clinical and agricultural technique for selecting motile sperm, but the specific molecular traits that define the successfully migrating subpopulation remain poorly characterized. In boars, where sperm handling and storage (e.g., NCSU media, centrifugation protocols) are critical for artificial insemination, understanding these traits is essential for improving fertility prediction. Pre-2026 work highlights capacitation and chromatin structure as key fertility determinants, yet their specific status in swim-up-selected sperm is not fully defined.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that boar spermatozoa successfully migrating in a swim-up procedure possess a distinct proteomic profile, superior chromatin compaction, and a heightened readiness for capacitation compared to the unselected population.
proposals.method: Fresh boar ejaculates will be processed using a standardized swim-up protocol in NCSU-23 medium. We will collect paired samples: the high-motility swim-up fraction (SU) and the raw semen (RS). Chromatin integrity will be assessed using flow cytometric sperm chromatin structure assay (SCSA) and toluidine blue staining. Capacitation status (tyrosine phosphorylation patterns) and proteomic differences will be analyzed via western blot and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) on sperm protein extracts. Statistical analyses (e.g., t-tests, PCA) will correlate molecular data with motility metrics.
proposals.contribution: This study will define a molecular signature of high-quality, migration-competent boar sperm, directly linking a routine selection method to underlying physiological states. It will provide specific protein biomarkers and chromatin quality thresholds that can be used to refine sperm processing protocols and improve fertility assessment in swine breeding.
proposals.novelty: While swim-up is widely used, a multi-parameter molecular analysis integrating proteomics, capacitation markers, and chromatin structure specifically in the boar model is lacking. This approach moves beyond simple motility assessment to define the functional biochemistry of the selected population.

proposals.title: Development of an Automated, High-Throughput Platform Integrating Flow Cytometric Sperm Analysis and Zona-Binding Assay (ZBA) for Industry-Wide Boar Fertility Prediction and Genetic Selection
proposals.motivation: In the swine industry, accurate and early prediction of boar fertility is critical for genetic improvement and herd productivity. Current methods for assessing sperm quality, such as manual morphology checks or single-parameter flow cytometry, are either low-throughput or insufficiently predictive of in vivo fertility outcomes, particularly capacitation status and zona pellucida binding ability. Scaling these assessments to process thousands of semen batches is a major bottleneck for implementing data-driven genetic selection programs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fully automated, integrated analytical platform combining multi-parametric flow cytometric assessment of sperm chromatin integrity, viability, and capacitation status with a high-throughput, automated Zona-Binding Assay (ZBA) will generate a robust fertility index capable of predicting field fertility outcomes for thousands of boar semen samples.
proposals.method: We will develop a robotic liquid handling system to automate sample preparation (extending from Rodriguez-Martinez's work on media and centrifugation). For analysis, we will implement a high-content flow cytometry panel assessing SYBR-14/PI viability, chromomycin A3 (CMA3) for chromatin compaction, Merocyanine 540 for membrane disorder, and Fluo-4 AM for calcium flux as a capacitation marker. Concurrently, we will automate the ZBA using biotinylated zona pellucida proteins immobilized on streptavidin-coated microplates, with sperm binding quantified by automated microscopy and image analysis via a convolutional neural network (CNN) for object detection and counting. Data from both streams will be integrated using a Random Forest regression algorithm to generate a composite fertility prediction score.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first fully automated, high-throughput platform for boar fertility assessment, directly linking advanced sperm function tests (chromatin and capacitation) with a key biological interaction (zona binding). It will provide the swine genetics industry with a scalable tool to phenotype thousands of sires, enabling more precise genetic selection for male fertility traits and improving the predictive value of semen quality evaluations.
proposals.novelty: While flow cytometry and ZBA exist separately, their integration into a single, automated pipeline for processing industrial-scale sample numbers is novel. The application of a CNN for automated ZBA scoring and the use of a machine learning model to fuse multi-parametric flow and binding data into a single fertility index represent significant methodological advancements beyond current practice.

proposals.title: Direct Validation of the Porcine Zona Pellucida Binding Assay: Correlating In Vitro Sperm-ZP Interaction with In Vivo Fertilization Outcomes
proposals.motivation: The in vitro zona pellucida (ZP) binding assay is a cornerstone for evaluating boar sperm capacitation status and fertility potential in research and clinical settings. However, its predictive value for actual in vivo fertilization success in pigs remains inadequately validated, creating uncertainty in translating laboratory findings to field applications. A direct, controlled comparison is needed to establish the assay's biological relevance and diagnostic utility for porcine reproduction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the proportion of spermatozoa binding to homologous ZP in vitro will show a strong positive correlation with fertilization rates and embryo quality following artificial insemination (AI) in sows. Specifically, ejaculates with high ZP-binding scores will yield superior in vivo outcomes.
proposals.method: Semen from 15 boars will be processed using standard NCSU-23 medium and centrifugation protocols. For each ejaculate, a detailed ZP-binding assay will be performed using salt-stored porcine oocytes, quantifying bound sperm per oocyte via microscopy. Parallel aliquots will be used for multiparametric flow cytometric analysis (membrane integrity, capacitation status, chromatin stability). Each ejaculate will then be used for intrauterine AI of 5 synchronized sows (n=75 sows total). Oviducts will be flushed 48 hours post-ovulation to recover zygotes/embryos for assessment of fertilization rate, polyspermy, and early cleavage stage.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust dataset directly correlating a standardized in vitro ZP-binding assay with in vivo fertilization success in pigs. It will clarify the assay's value as a predictor of male fertility and identify which complementary sperm quality parameters (e.g., chromatin status, flow cytometry metrics) best explain any observed discrepancies between in vitro binding and in vivo function.
proposals.novelty: While ZP-binding assays are widely used, a systematic validation against direct in vivo fertilization outcomes in a controlled, homologous porcine model is lacking. This study uniquely integrates precise in vitro assays with a direct in vivo fertilization endpoint, moving beyond correlations with farrowing rates to a more mechanistic understanding of the assay's predictive power.

proposals.title: Targeted Semen Extender Formulation Based on Flow Cytometric Fertility-Associated Phenotypes to Preserve Boar Sperm Capacitation Potential and Chromatin Integrity
proposals.motivation: Commercial boar semen extenders are designed for general sperm survival, not for preserving specific high-fertility traits. Pre-2026 flow cytometric analyses have identified key functional phenotypes (e.g., high mitochondrial membrane potential, stable chromatin, intact acrosomes) strongly correlated with field fertility, but these traits degrade at different rates during storage. A one-size-fits-all extender may not optimally maintain this suite of critical characteristics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel extender, formulated by targeting the specific biochemical vulnerabilities of high-value sperm subpopulations identified via multi-parametric flow cytometry, will better preserve the capacitation potential and chromatin integrity of boar spermatozoa during liquid storage compared to standard commercial extenders.
proposals.method: Semen from high-fertility boars will be analyzed using a pre-2026 validated flow cytometry panel (YO-PRO-1/PI, MitoTracker Deep Red, JC-1, Monobromobimane for thiol status, anti-phosphotyrosine for capacitation). A phenotypic fingerprint of high-fertility sperm will be defined. Using a Design of Experiments (DoE) approach, we will systematically test extender components (antioxidants, energy substrates, chelators, pH buffers) known from pre-2026 literature to influence these traits. The response variables for optimization will be the preservation of the defined high-fertility phenotype and in vitro capacitation response after 72h of storage at 17°C in NCSU-23-based media.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from generic semen preservation towards trait-specific, precision-based extender design. It will provide a validated, phenotype-driven protocol for formulating extenders that directly target the sperm attributes most predictive of successful fertilization and litter size, potentially improving the efficiency of swine artificial insemination.
proposals.novelty: While extender optimization is common, the novelty lies in using a multi-parametric, flow-cytometry-defined 'fertility phenotype' as the direct blueprint and success metric for formulation, rather than relying solely on gross motility or membrane integrity.

ideas.title: Protocol Inversion to Probe Heterogeneous Cryo-Damage Subpopulations
ideas.description: This study fuses the concepts of protocol inversion and heterogeneous cryo-damage to test the hypothesis that the order of cooling and synthetic liquid crystal (SLC) application acts as a selective filter, exposing pre-existing subpopulations with differential biophysical resilience. By applying SLC before cooling and comparing it to the standard protocol, we will not just assess mean survival but will use high-throughput single-cell analysis (e.g., kinematics, OMICS, membrane integrity) to track if the two protocols produce fundamentally different distributions of damage types and severities. This approach functionally classifies sperm into distinct cohorts—such as those vulnerable to cold shock versus those vulnerable to SLC-induced osmotic stress—based on their survival trajectory. The result is a mechanistic link between procedural sequence and the unmasking of cryptic heterogeneity, guiding the development of tailored, subpopulation-specific preservation strategies.

ideas.description: This approach reframes the SLC waste fraction not as a source of defective sperm to be rescued, but as a reservoir of sperm with potentially non-standard phenotypes that were not prioritized by the original selection criteria. A high-content analysis platform continuously profiles the discarded population for unconventional but potentially beneficial traits, such as unique motility patterns or resilience markers. Identified candidates are individually isolated and undergo a rapid, targeted genomic screen to confirm baseline genetic integrity, disqualifying those with excessive DNA damage. This creates a validated, alternative sperm pool for use in cases where standard SLC-selected sperm have repeatedly failed, offering a data-driven second line of selection without compromising safety.

ideas.title: Integrated Sperm Processing and Viability Assessment via Microfluidic Lab-on-a-Chip
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solutions from environmental microbiology, specifically integrated biosensors for in-situ water quality assessment. The analogous problem involves needing to both concentrate and functionally assess microbial populations from a complex sample in a single, automated workflow. The adapted solution is to develop a microfluidic 'lab-on-a-chip' platform for IVF. The chip would integrate sequential modules: first, a clean-up stage using passive physical or biochemical principles to isolate motile, morphologically normal sperm from seminal plasma and debris. Immediately downstream, the processed sperm would flow into functional assessment zones with embedded biosensors. These sensors would measure key physiological parameters indicative of sperm health and fertilizing capacity in real-time, providing a comprehensive, automated profile from a single sample loading. This creates an integrated clean-up and assessment protocol that minimizes handling and time delays, mirroring the efficiency of field-deployable environmental monitors.

ideas.title: Adapting Microfluidic Cell Sorting for High-Throughput Donkey Sperm Processing
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from human clinical diagnostics to solve the scaling problem in donkey semen processing. The analogous field is Human Clinical Diagnostics and Cell Biology, where high-throughput microfluidic cell sorters process millions of cells using parallelized channels, integrated sensors, and automated sorting. The adapted idea is to develop an automated, large-volume SLC system for commercial AI centers by designing a device with multiple parallel microfluidic SLC channels. Each channel processes a single donkey ejaculate simultaneously. Integrated optical sensors will provide real-time analysis of sperm quality (motility, morphology), enabling the system to automatically sort processed samples into quality-graded batches for artificial insemination, dramatically increasing throughput.

ideas.title: Recovering Rare Sperm Phenotypes from SLC Waste via Mineralogical Ore Refinement Analogy
ideas.description: This idea applies the mineral processing solution of re-mining industrial waste tailings to recover valuable resources to the problem of recovering viable sperm from the discarded fraction after Sperm Linear Collimation (SLC). In mining, tailings are re-processed with advanced, targeted methods to extract valuable minerals. Adapted to this context, the research involves analyzing the SLC 'waste' fraction not as a homogeneous discard, but as a low-grade 'ore' containing rare, viable sperm phenotypes. By implementing a high-throughput, multi-parameter phenotypic screening platform (e.g., imaging flow cytometry) tuned to identify sperm with non-standard but potentially advantageous characteristics (e.g., specific morphokinetic signatures or resilience markers), we can develop protocols to rescue these valuable subpopulations, effectively solving the inverse problem of sperm recovery from SLC waste.

ideas.description: This research applies a deep learning-based computer vision system, trained for donkey sperm morphology, to perform detailed morphometric analysis on ejaculates before and after Single Layer Centrifugation (SLC). The core objective is to test the hypothesis that SLC can be detrimental to certain high-quality samples by using the AI not just for scoring, but to discover subtle, pre-processing morphological patterns that predict susceptibility to SLC-induced damage. This will establish if ejaculates with excellent conventional metrics but specific AI-identified morphometric profiles suffer a decline in quality post-SLC. The outcome is a data-driven decision framework to selectively bypass SLC for ejaculates predicted to be harmed by it, optimizing processing protocols based on individual sample characteristics.

ideas.title: Single-Cell Multi-Omic Profiling of Cryopreserved Sperm to Map Heterogeneous Damage Subtypes
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that cryopreservation inflicts uniform damage across all spermatozoa, proposing instead that it creates heterogeneous subpopulations with distinct damage profiles. Drawing an analogy from cancer biology, where single-cell omics shattered the myth of tumor uniformity to reveal critical functional subtypes, we adapt this solution to sperm cryobiology. The adapted idea is to apply single-cell multi-omic profiling (e.g., scRNA-seq combined with single-cell assays for DNA, membranes, and organelles) to post-thaw sperm populations. By clustering this high-dimensional data, we aim to identify and characterize specific 'cryo-damage subtypes,' moving beyond a simple normal/abnormal binary. This will establish a new framework for understanding differential sperm cryo-survival and allow for the development of subtype-specific cryoprotection strategies to improve assisted reproductive technology outcomes.

ideas.description: This study moves beyond a simple test of SLC on excellent samples by first rigorously defining 'high-quality' using a multi-parameter clinical benchmark. It then employs a two-tiered experimental design to detect subtle or conditional benefits. The first tier assesses if SLC maintains baseline quality during standard processing. The second, crucial tier subjects processed samples to a standardized in vitro stressor to test the hypothesis that SLC's primary benefit for top-tier ejaculates is not improving baseline metrics, but enhancing resilience to subsequent preservation or environmental insults, a previously unmeasured outcome.

ideas.title: Predicting In Vivo Fertility via a Multi-Parameter In Vitro Diagnostic Index
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from oncology drug screening, where in vitro multi-parameter profiles predict in vivo efficacy. The analogous problem is predicting a complex biological outcome (fertility) from simplified in vitro assays. The adapted idea is to challenge the assumption that single sperm assays are predictive by creating a multi-parameter in vitro fertility index. This involves high-throughput functional testing of sperm samples across a battery of assays mimicking key in vivo steps, then using statistical and machine learning models to correlate these integrated profiles with documented fertility outcomes from field trials, thereby creating a validated predictive diagnostic tool.

ideas.title: Species-specific deep learning for automated donkey sperm morphology analysis
ideas.description: This research develops a computer vision system specifically for donkey sperm morphology by first establishing a gold-standard, species-specific morphological dataset. Expert andrologists will annotate thousands of donkey sperm images post-SLC to define normal and abnormal morphological criteria unique to equids. This curated dataset is then used to train a deep learning model, ensuring its classifications are biologically valid for donkeys. The final system provides automated, objective scoring with integrated uncertainty flags, creating a robust tool tailored for donkey reproduction management and research.

ideas.title: Dynamic Compartment-Specific Redox Mapping and Targeted Antioxidant Pathway Analysis to Elucidate SLC's Protective Mechanism
ideas.description: This redesigned study moves beyond static bulk assays to dynamically visualize and quantify oxidative stress within specific, functionally relevant cellular compartments of spermatozoa, such as the mitochondria and the cytosol surrounding the axoneme. It employs live-cell imaging with compartment-targeted redox-sensitive fluorescent proteins to track real-time changes in redox state upon challenge, directly linking SLC exposure to the preservation of physiological redox signaling versus the mitigation of pathological oxidative damage. Concurrently, it profiles the activity of specific antioxidant enzyme pathways and measures definitive lipid peroxidation end-products to identify the precise molecular mechanisms through which SLC exerts its protective effect, distinguishing between direct scavenging and upregulation of endogenous defense systems.

ideas.title: Adapting a universal pharmaceutical formulation approach to optimize Androcoll-E-Large for diverse donkey breeds
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a single colloid formulation (Androcoll-E-Large) is optimal for all donkey breeds by drawing an analogy from human pharmacology. In drug development, a universal formulation often fails due to genetic differences in target populations; the solution involves platform-based development followed by tailored variants for specific genetic subgroups. Adapted to this context, the research would first map the efficacy of the standard Androcoll-E-Large across diverse donkey breeds with known sperm phenotypic and genetic differences (e.g., Poitou, Mammoth Jack, Catalan). Analyzing the response variability would then inform the development of a small set of optimized colloid variants—adjusting parameters like density or polymer composition—tailored to the needs of major breed clusters, thereby creating an effective 'family' of solutions from a common technological platform rather than insisting on a single one-size-fits-all product.
